EIS Test \n\nThis test used a tool in the form of a flat cell which had function as a medium for \n\nelectrochemical cells to enable measurements of electrochemical impedance in various \n\nelectrochemical systems (Lazanas and Prodromidis , 2023) . In Figure 3, the Nyquist plo t from each \n\nsample form ed a scatter plot. The Ny qui st plot was useful for determining the stability of a \n\nsystem because it show ed the location of the poles and zeros of the system in the complex \n\nplane. Bode plot, was a plot of magnitude and phase function transfer system as a function of \n\nfrequency. \n\nIn this study, we used Nyquist plot because it showed the location of the poles and zeros. \n\nThe threshold value of chi -squared (\u03c72) was: (1) if the value of \u03c7 2 < 10 -6 indicated that the fitting \n\nresults were very suitable, (2) if the value of 10 -6 < \u03c72 < 10 -5, it was quite reasonable, (3) if the value \n\nof 10 -5 < \u03c7 2 < 10 -4 then it could be included in the acceptance threshold, and (4) if the value of \u03c7 2 >\n\n10 -4 then it was a bad value and could not be used as data interpretation (Lazanas and \n\nProdromidis , 2023) . Bas ed on this, it can be seen that none of the chi -squared values for each \n\nsample met the threshold . In this way, we can be sure that there were errors occurring in the \n\nresulting data, so that the resulting data could be said to be invalid and unusable. Usual ly, the \n\nscatter plot formed on a Nyquist graph was caused by non -uniformity of the coating layer, such \n\nas: uneven coating layer thickness, defects in the substrate, or impurities still attached to the \n\nsubstrate surface (Varacalle et al ., 2006) . The non -uniformity of the layer c ould cause variations \n\nin the impedance response at different measurement points, resulting in a scatter plot being \n\nformed on the Nyquist graph. \n\nTo analyse the corrosion resistance of the coating layer in protecting ASTM A36 ste el \n\nsubstrates from corrosive environments, 2 testing methods were applied , namely the salt spray \n\nmethod and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In the salt spray test, the results \n\nshowed that the abrasive blast cleaning method and power tool to b are metal cleaning were the \n\nsurface preparation methods with the highest level of effectiveness in preventing corrosion with \n\nchanges in the width of the scratches formed by 0.82 and 0.97 \u03bcm. Both were included in rating \n\nnumber 8. Meanwhile, in the EIS test , a randomly arranged pattern (scatter plot) was produced. \n\nThis ha d an i mpa ct on the resulting chi -square value which was also high (Ikani et al ., 2024) . When \n\nthe chi -square value was large, it indicated that the discrepancy between the experimental data \n\nand the predicted data from the equivalent circuit model was also large, and hence it could be \n\nascertained that the possibility of errors occurring in the resulting data was very large (Dong et \n\nal ., 2023; Momber et al ., 2024) .23     \n\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n> (a) (b) (c)\n> (d) (e)\n\nFigure 3 EIS result s of (a) SC; (b) HTC; (c) PTC; (d) PTBMC; and (e) ABC samples \n\n3.6. outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:1201:outputs\codex_findings\v23_regression_diagnosis\_codex_stdout.txt:6803:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:916:      "content": "Abstract\nThere is growing awareness of the environmental presence of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) and their harmful effects on animals and humans. Recent studies have revealed changes in human embryonic stem cells and maternal biomarkers, underscoring the severity and unpredictable outcomes associated with long-term exposure to PFAS. Monitoring efforts continually identify additional PFAS compounds worldwide, but a standardized and unified approach is still lacking. Traditional treatment methods such as adsorption and membrane filtration have been effective in removing 80–95% of PFAS from wastewater. However, complete removal of short-chain PFAS remains limited to a few recently developed techniques. The inability of advanced treatment methods to eliminate emerging short-chain and ultrashort-chain PFAS suggests the need for more integrated approaches that target all PFAS classes. Additionally, a few studies have discussed the potential toxicity outcomes of these treatments at both laboratory and full-scale levels. While advanced oxidative processes (AOPs) are rapidly gaining attention for degrading 90–100% of PFAS in sewage, it remains challenging to fully break down PFAS into non-toxic, mineralized products such as CO2 and H2O due to the strong C-F bonds and the potential toxicity of by-products in post-treated wastewater. Standardized and reliable bioassays for assessing PFAS toxicity are still under development, and current predictive models linking molecular structure to human health effects are at an early stage. This review examines the emerging health and ecological risks associated with both legacy and novel PFAS, alongside recent advances and limitations in individual and combined treatment technologies for water and wastewater. Emphasis is placed on the potential toxicity of degradation products, highlighting the need for more integrated and comprehensive toxicity assessments to guide safer PFAS remediation strategies.\nSupplementary Information\nThe online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s40068-025-00411-9.\nKeywords: Review, PFAS, Monitoring, Treatment technologies, Toxicity\nIntroduction\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are industrially manufactured organofluorine chemical compounds with stable carbon–fluorine bonds (Gaines 2023). Polymeric and non-polymeric PFAS are the major classes reported in the literature (Buck et al., 2011). Fluorinated polymers consist of high-molecular-weight compounds with carbon-based backbones that are partially or fully substituted with fluorine atoms (Buck et al., 2011). The nonpolymeric PFAS, which are subclassified into perfluoralkyl (fully fluorinated) and polyfluoralkyl (partially fluorinated) substances, include a broad range of chemicals with various functional groups such as perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs), perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs), perfluoroalkyl sulfonamides (PFSNs), fluorotelomer-based compounds (FT-PFAS such as FTOHs), as well as semi-fluorinated aliphatic compounds such as alkanes and alkenes, and other related derivatives (Buck et al., 2011). Fluorotelomer alcohols (FTOHs) are key precursors that can degrade into PFCAs and, under some conditions, into PFSAs. Structural representations of some of these classes are provided in Fig. S1 (Buck et al., 2011; Dickman & Aga 2022; Glüge et al. 2020).\nThese compounds possess various physicochemical properties, including chemical inertness, strong and short C-F bonds, excellent thermal resistance, and surfactant properties (Brunn et al. 2023; Gaines 2023; Naidu et al. 2025; Rayne & Forest 2009). The carbon chain length influences their physicochemical behavior, such as hydrophobicity, bioaccumulation potential, and environmental mobility (Gaines 2023; Glüge et al. 2020; Kissa & Kissa 2001; Rayne & Forest 2009). Although there is no defined upper limit to the carbon chain length of PFAS, research has predominantly focused on compounds with C4 to C14 chains (Brunn et al. 2023; Kissa & Kissa 2001). Longer-chain PFAS, such as those with C16 carbon atoms, have been reported but remain less extensively studied (Liu et al. 2018). As per US EPA classification, PFAS are classified by chain length as either short-chain, with fewer than six carbon atoms for PFSAs and fewer than eight for PFCAs, or long-chain, with six or more carbon atoms for PFSAs and eight or more for PFCAs (Li et al. 2020; Podder et al. 2021). Shorter PFAS are shown to exhibit higher hydrophilicity and water solubility compared to their longer alternatives (Li et al. 2020). Most PFAS are anionic surfactants and can have pKa values as low as 0.4 (perfluorobutane sulfonic acid, PFBS’s pKa) and a mean pKa value of 3.8 ± 1 (perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA’s pKa) (Burns et al. 2008; Naidu et al. 2025; Rayne & Forest 2009). Glüge et al. (2020) categorized PFAS uses into several groups based on their structure–function relationships. This structural versatility has enabled diverse industrial applications, which in turn have contributed to their widespread occurrence in surface water, groundwater, drinking water, and ecosystems globally (Glüge et al. 2020). The environmental presence of PFAS was first reported by Giesy and Kannan in 2001 (Giesy & Kannan 2001). Since then, numerous studies have documented a wide range of PFAS classes in both environmental and biological matrices (Muir & Miaz 2021). Perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) have historically been among the most extensively studied PFAS worldwide (Gallen et al. 2017; Giesy & Kannan, 2002; Podder et al. 2021). However, due to their environmental persistence and well-documented adverse health effects, these compounds have been phased out or are now subject to regulatory restrictions across multiple jurisdictions (Dettori et al. 2022; Podder et al. 2021State of Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) Report, 2025).\nPFAS are often referred to as “forever chemicals” because of the inherent physicochemical properties of carbon–fluorine bonds, which resist breakdown under typical environmental conditions (Brunn et al. 2023; Wee & Aris 2023b). Consequently, they frequently bioaccumulate in the body, bio-magnify through food chains, leading to dietary exposure in humans (Brunn et al. 2023). Mounting evidence has consistently shown the adverse health effects of PFAS, especially PFOA and PFOS, on humans, animals, and ecological systems with epidemiologic studies linking them to a wide range of harmful effects, including developmental toxicity, liver damage, fat metabolic toxicity, heart disease, immune system suppression, endocrine disruption, and kidney or testicular cancer (Ankley et al. 2020; Brunn et al. 2023; Das et al. 2015; Domingo & Nadal 2019; Donat-Vargas et al. 2019; Fenton et al. 2021; Steenland & Winquist 2021; Von Holst et al. 2021).\nIn response to continuous efforts to address the PFAS issue, an international guideline was established in 2019 to achieve a very low limit of quantification (LOQ) in water for at least 30 different classes of PFAS (International Organization for Standardization., 2019). The ISO 21675 standard, issued by the International Organization for Standardization, establishes a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.0002 mg/L or lower for the majority of the listed PFAS compounds (International Organization for Standardization., 2019). However, this varies by country and depends on the analytical methods used. In Canada, updated drafts were published on PFAS regulatory limits ( Health Canada, 2018a, 2018b). Maximum acceptable concentrations (MACs) for the PFOA and PFOS were set to 0.200 and 0.600 mg/L, respectively (Health Canada, 2018a , 2018b). MACs have been devised based on the length of human exposure to the pollutants, including children. The Canadian guidelines recently included MACs for another nine PFAS detected in drinking water (Health Canada, 2018a , 2018b). According to the guidelines, the total PFAS in any drinking water sample should not exceed 0.030 mg/L (Health Canada, 2024a). US EPA guidelines also restrict the presence of PFAS in drinking water to 0.0004 mg/L (EPA 2021). European Union (EU) guidelines have also limited the concentration of all PFAS in any sample to 0.500 mg/L and the sum of 20 selected PFAS to less than 0.100 mg/L (Table S1) (Dettori et al. 2022). The US EPA and Environment and Climate Change Canada have recently added fluorotelomer sulfonic acids (FTSA) due to their associations with several health effects in humans and animals ( Health Canada, 2024b).\nTo reduce PFAS in the environment, various remedial strategies targeting PFAS in wastewater have been developed, with particular emphasis on combined treatment technologies (Lu et al. 2020; Wanninayake 2021). The versatility of these combined approaches has contributed not only to higher removal efficiencies but also to a better understanding of PFAS remediation pathways in contaminated water. Among these, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) coupled with adsorption have attracted considerable attention as a practical strategy for breaking down PFAS into simpler molecules through defluorination and eventual mineralization (Lu et al. 2020). Although these technologies have demonstrated promising removal performance, recent studies suggest that the risk levels in post-treated water may be higher than in pre-treated water, likely due to the formation of more toxic degradation byproducts (Liu et al. 2024). This review highlights the recent global monitoring of PFAS, advances in toxicity assessment, and the emerging potential of combined treatment technologies to reduce PFAS contamination in water and other environmental systems. Importantly, evaluating the toxicity of treated water is a critical step toward developing effective and sustainable solutions to mitigate the harmful effects of PFAS.\nGlobal monitoring of PFAS and their sources\nAlthough the industrial production of PFAS dates back to the 1950s,"

outputs\codex_findings\v29_deep_content_audit\_codex_stdout.txt:458:   9: Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common treatment-emergent adverse effects with tirzepatide, with nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting being dose-dependent and typically mild to moderate in severity.[3] Real-world data from a disproportionality analysis of the FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) confirmed common events included nausea (n=2,519) and diarrhea (n=1,111).[8] The risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide is low when used without insulin or insulin secretagogues but increases when combined with these agents.[3] The same trial reported blood glucose less than 54 mg/dL or severe hypoglycemia in 10.6% of the tirzepatide-pooled group versus 48.0% with insulin lispro.[9] A FAERS analysis identified 115 reports of hypoglycemia with a signal strength (EB05) of 4.44.[8] The FAERS analysis identified 190 reports of pancreatitis (EB05=6.85) and signals for gallbladder disorder (n=38, EB05=3.48) and cholecystitis (n=18, EB05=2.00).[8] Injection-site reactions are commonly reported, with FAERS data showing strong signals for injection-site pain (n=2,793), injection-site hemorrhage (n=1,267), and injection-site coldness (EB05=42.61).[8] A disproportionality analysis of FAERS data from 2004-2024 found a positive association between tirzepatide and thyroid cancer (ROR=2.09, 95% CI: 1.51–2.89), a finding echoed in another report noting an ROR of 2.09.[10][11] However, the clinical relevance in humans remains uncertain, leading to an active surveillance program, the Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma Surveillance Study, to monitor for a potential signal in the U.S. population.[12]

outputs\codex_findings\v29_deep_content_audit\_codex_stdout.txt:3967:   9: Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common treatment-emergent adverse effects with tirzepatide, with nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting being dose-dependent and typically mild to moderate in severity.[3] Real-world data from a disproportionality analysis of the FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) confirmed common events included nausea (n=2,519) and diarrhea (n=1,111).[8] The risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide is low when used without insulin or insulin secretagogues but increases when combined with these agents.[3] The same trial reported blood glucose less than 54 mg/dL or severe hypoglycemia in 10.6% of the tirzepatide-pooled group versus 48.0% with insulin lispro.[9] A FAERS analysis identified 115 reports of hypoglycemia with a signal strength (EB05) of 4.44.[8] The FAERS analysis identified 190 reports of pancreatitis (EB05=6.85) and signals for gallbladder disorder (n=38, EB05=3.48) and cholecystitis (n=18, EB05=2.00).[8] Injection-site reactions are commonly reported, with FAERS data showing strong signals for injection-site pain (n=2,793), injection-site hemorrhage (n=1,267), and injection-site coldness (EB05=42.61).[8] A disproportionality analysis of FAERS data from 2004-2024 found a positive association between tirzepatide and thyroid cancer (ROR=2.09, 95% CI: 1.51–2.89), a finding echoed in another report noting an ROR of 2.09.[10][11] However, the clinical relevance in humans remains uncertain, leading to an active surveillance program, the Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma Surveillance Study, to monitor for a potential signal in the U.S. population.[12]

Please note that whilst every effort has been made to ensure the accuracy of this NORSOK standard, neither OLF nor TBL or any of their members will assume liability for any use thereof. Standards Norway is responsible for the administration and publication of this NORSOK standard. Standards Norway Telephone: + 47 67 83 86 00 Strandveien 18, P.O. Box 242 Fax: + 47 67 83 86 01 N-1326 Lysaker Email: petroleum@standard.no NORWAY Website: www.standard.no/petroleum Copyrights reserved \n\n# NORSOK STANDARD M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 Surface preparation and protective coating NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 1 of 20 \n\nForeword 2Introduction 21 Scope 32 Normative and informative references 3\n\n2.1 Normative references 32.2 Informative references 4\n\n3 Terms, definitions and abbreviations 4\n\n3.1 Terms and definitions 43.2 Abbreviations 5\n\n4 General requirements 5\n\n4.1 General 54.2 Planning 54.3 Equipment protection and clean up 54.4 Ambient conditions 54.5 Coating materials 54.6 Steel materials 64.7 Shop primer 64.8 Unpainted surfaces 64.9 Handling and shipping of coated items 64.10 Pre-qualification of products, personnel and procedures 74.11 Metal coating 74.12 Records and reports 7\n\n5 Health, safety and environment 76 Surface preparation 7\n\n6.1 Pre-blasting preparations 76.2 Blast cleaning 76.3 Final surface condition 8\n\n7 Paint application 8\n\n7.1 General 87.2 Application equipment 87.3 Application 8\n\n8 Thermally sprayed metallic coatings 9\n\n8.1 General 98.2 Coating materials 98.3 Application of thermally sprayed coating 98.4 Repair, field coating of pipes and coating of in-fill steel 9\n\n9 Sprayed on passive fire protection 9\n\n9.1 General 99.2 Materials 99.3 Application 10 9.4 Repairs 10 \n\n10 Qualification requirements 10 \n\n10.1 Pre-qualification of products 10 10.2 Qualification of companies and personnel 12 10.3 Qualification of procedures 13 \n\n11 Inspection and testing 14 Annex A (Normative) Coating systems 15 Annex B (Informative) Colours 20 NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 2 of 20 \n\n## Foreword \n\nThe NORSOK standards are developed by the Norwegian petroleum industry to ensure adequate safety, value adding and cost effectiveness for petroleum industry developments and operations. Furthermore, NORSOK standards are as far as possible intended to replace oil company specifications and serve as references in the authorities\u2019 regulations. The NORSOK standards are normally based on recognised international standards, adding the provisions deemed necessary to fill the broad needs of the Norwegian petroleum industry. Where relevant, NORSOK standards will be used to provide the Norwegian industry input to the international standardisation process. Subject to development and publication of international standards, the relevant NORSOK standard will be withdrawn. The NORSOK standards are developed according to the consensus principle generally applicable standards work and according to established procedures defined in NORSOK A-001. The NORSOK standards are prepared and published with support by The Norwegian Oil Industry Association (OLF) and Federation of Norwegian Manufacturing Industries (TBL). NORSOK standards are administered and published by Standards Norway. Annex A is normative, whilst Annex B is for information only. \n\n## Introduction \n\nThe main changes included in this revision are that \n\nx ISO 20340 has been adopted as pre-qualification test standard for coating systems; \n\nx fire testing of aged spray-on passive fire protection has been introduced; \n\nx minimum requirements with respect to coating products, number of coats and coating thickness have been introduced also for coating systems that shall be pre-qualified; \n\nx DNV classification testing has been accepted as an alternative qualification method for ballast water tank coatings (coating system no. 3B). Other changes and modifications are also made. All main changes are highlighted with revision marks. NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 3 of 20 \n\n## 1 Scope \n\nThis NORSOK standard gives the requirements for the selection of coating materials, surface preparation, application procedures and inspection for protective coatings to be applied during the construction and installation of offshore installations and associated facilities. This NORSOK standard cover both paints, metallic coatings and application of spray-on passive fire protective coatings. The aim of this NORSOK standard is to obtain a coating system, which ensures \n\nx optimal protection of the installation with a minimum need for maintenance; \n\nx that the coating system is maintenance friendly; \n\nx that the coating system is application friendly; \n\nx that health, safety and environmental impacts are evaluated and documented. This NORSOK standard is not applicable to pipelines and pipeline risers. \n\n## 2 Normative and informative references \n\nThe following standards include provisions and guidelines which, through reference in this text, constitute provisions and guidelines of this NORSOK standard. Latest issue of the references shall be used unless otherwise agreed. Other recognized standards may be used provided it can be shown that they meet or exceed the requirements and guidelines of the standards referenced below. \n\n2.1 Normative references \n\nASTM D4752 Standard Test Method for Measuring MEK Resistance of Ethyl Silicate (Inorganic) Zinc-Rich Primers by Solvent Rub. DIN 8566-2 Zus\u00e4tze f\u00fcr das termische Spritzen; Massivdr\u00e4hte zum Lichtbogenspritzen; Technische Lieferbedingungen. DNV Classification Note 33.1 Corrosion prevention of tanks and holds. ISO 1461 Metallic coatings - Hot-dip galvanised coating on fabricated ferrous products -Requirements. ISO 2814 Paints and varnishes - Comparison of contrast ratio (hiding power) of paint of the same type and colour. ISO 4624 Paints and varnishes - Pull-off test for adhesion. ISO 4628-6 Paints and varnishes - Evaluation of degradation of paint coatings - Designation of intensity, quantity and size of common types of defect \u2013 Part 6: Rating of degree of chalking by tape method. ISO 8501-1 Preparation of steel substrates before application of paints and related products -Visual assessment of surface cleanliness \u2013 Part 1: Rust grades and preparation grades of uncoated steel substrates and of steel substrates after overall removal of previous coatings. Informative supplement to part 1: Representative photographic examples of the change of appearance imparted to steel when blast-cleaned with different abrasives (ISO 8501-1:1988/Suppl: 1994). ISO 8502-3 Preparation of steel substrates before application of paints and related products - Test for the assessment of surface cleanliness \u2013 Part 3: Assessment of dust on steel surfaces prepared for painting (pressure sensitive tape method). ISO 8502-6 Preparation of steel substrates before application of paints and related products - Test for the assessment of surface cleanliness \u2013 Part 6: Extraction of soluble contaminants for analysis - The Bresle method. ISO 8502-9 Preparation of steel substrates before application of paints and related products - Test for the assessment of surface cleanliness \u2013 Part 9: Field method for the conductometric determination of water-soluble salts. ISO 8503 Preparation of steel substrates before application of paints and related products -Surface roughness characteristics of blast cleaned substrates. ISO 8504-2 Preparation of steel substrates before application of paints and related products -Surface preparation methods \u2013 Part 2: Abrasive blast cleaning. NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 4 of 20 ISO 12944-5 Paints and varnishes \u2013 Corrosion protection of steel structures by protective paint systems \u2013 Part 5: Protective paint systems. ISO 19840 Paints and varnishes \u2013 Corrosion protection of steel structures by protective paint systems \u2013 Measurement of, and acceptance criteria for, the thickness of dry film on rough surfaces. ISO 20340 Paints and varnishes \u2013 Performance requirements for protective paint systems for offshore and related structures. NACE RP0188 Discontinuity (holiday) testing of protective coatings. NORSOK M-001 Material selection. NORSOK S-002 Working environment. NS 476 Paints and coatings - Approval and certification of surface treatment inspectors. SFS 8145 Anticorrosive painting, surface preparation methods of blast cleaned and shop primer coated steel substrates and preparation grades for respective treatments. SSPC/SSPM Volume 2 Systems and Specifications, Fourth Edition. \n\n2.2 Informative references \n\nDIN 32521 Acceptance test and quality control for thermal spraying equipment. \n\n## 3 Terms, definitions and abbreviations \n\nFor the purposes of this NORSOK standard, the following terms, definitions and abbreviations apply. \n\n3.1 Terms and definitions 3.1.1 can \n\nverbal form used for statements of possibility and capability, whether material, physical or casual \n\n3.1.2 feathered \n\ngradual taper in thickness from a coated surface to an uncoated surface \n\n3.1.3 holiday \n\ndiscontinuity in a coating, which exhibits electrical conductivity, when exposed to a specific voltage \n\n3.1.4 may \n\nverbal form used to indicate a course of action permissible within the limits of the standard \n\n3.1.5 shall \n\nverbal form used to indicate requirements strictly to be followed in order to conform to the standard and from which no deviation is permitted, unless accepted by all involved parties \n\n3.1.6 should \n\nverbal form used to indicate that among several possibilities one is recommended as particularly suitable, without mentioning or excluding others, or that a certain course of action is preferred but not necessarily required \n\n3.1.7 shop primer \n\nthin protective coating normally applied for protection during transport and storage \n\n3.1.8 stripe coat \n\nsupplementary coat applied to ensure adequate protection of critical areas like edges, welds etc. NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 5 of 20 \n\n3.2 Abbreviations \n\nCPS coating procedure specification CPT coating procedure test CSDS coating system data sheet MDFT minimum dry film thickness NACE National Association of Corrosion Engineers NDFT nominal dry film thickness OAR occupational air requirements RAL colour definitions issued by RAL (Deutsches Institut f\u00fcr G\u00fctesicherung und Kennzeichnung e.V.) SSPC Steel Structures Painting Council SSPM Steel Structures Painting Manual QC quality control VOC volatile organic components \n\n## 4 General requirements 4.1 General \n\nSelection of coating systems and application procedures shall be made with due consideration to conditions during fabrication, installation and service of the installation. Specific qualification requirements for products, personnel and companies are given in Clause 10. \n\n4.2 Planning \n\nAll activities shall be fully incorporated in the fabrication plan. Details of management, inspectors, operators, facilities, equipment and qualified procedures shall be established and documented before commencing work. Steel surfaces shall be blast cleaned and coated (i.e. metal sprayed or coated with primer and the succeeding coat of the applicable coating system) prior to installation. \n\n4.3 Equipment protection and clean up \n\nAll equipment and structures shall be fully protected from mechanical damages, ingress of abrasives and dust from blast cleaning. Sags, droplets and paint over-spray (inclusive dry-spray) shall be avoided. Adjacent areas not to be painted or already finished shall be protected. On completion of the work in any area, all masking materials, spent abrasives, equipment etc. shall be removed. \n\n4.4 Ambient conditions \n\nNo final blast cleaning or coating application shall be done if the relative humidity is more than 85 % and when the steel temperature is less than 3 \u00b0C above the dew point. Coating shall only be applied or cured at ambient and steel temperatures above 0 \u00b0C. The coating manufacturer shall specify the maximum and minimum application and curing temperature and other relevant limitations regarding application and curing conditions for each product in any coating system. \n\n4.5 Coating materials \n\nThe selected coating materials shall be suitable for the intended use and shall be selected after an evaluation of all relevant aspects such as \n\nx corrosion protective properties; \n\nx requirements to health, safety and environment; \n\nx properties related to application conditions, equipment and personnel; \n\nx experience with the coating materials and coating system; \n\nx availability and economics of coating materials. All coating materials and solvents shall be protected from ignition sources and shall remain within storage temperatures and storage conditions recommended by the coating manufacturer. NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 6 of 20 All coating materials and solvents shall be stored in the original container bearing the manufacturer's label and instructions. Each product shall have a batch number showing year and month of manufacture and giving full traceability of production. Shelf life shall be included in the technical data sheet. Applicable coating systems are tabulated in Annex A. Coating system no. 1, 3B, 4, 5 and 7 shall be pre-qualified in accordance with Clause 10. For those coating systems which are subject to pre-qualification, the specified coating systems are examples, and alternative coating systems may be used if the requirements of this NORSOK standard are fulfilled. However, for coating system no. 1 and 7, the number of coats and the coating film thicknesses given in Annex A are minimum requirements which shall apply for coating systems subject to pre-qualification testing. Topcoat colours should be in accordance with Annex B. Light colours shall be used in ballast and fresh water tanks. \n\n4.6 Steel materials \n\nSteel subject to surface preparation on site shall as a minimum requirement be in accordance with rust-grade B according to ISO 8501-1. Shop primers shall be regarded as temporary corrosion protection and shall be removed prior to the application of the coating systems herein unless the requirements in 4.7 are fulfilled .\n\n4.7 Shop primer \n\nIf a shop primer is considered left on the surface to form an integrated part of the final coating system, it shall be applied in accordance with the following requirements: Blast cleaning shall comply with ISO 8501-1 Sa 2\u00bd and the surface shall remain at Sa 2\u00bd until application of the primer. The primer shall consist of 1 coat zinc ethyl silicate primer with 15 Pmthickness. Measured on a plane polished steel or glass test plate, the dry film thickness shall be maximum 25 Pm. Use of shop primer as an integrated part of the final coating system, can only be considered for coating system no. 1, 3B, 4, 5, 7 and 8. For coating system no. 1, 3B, 4, 5 and 7, the pre-qualification requirements in 10.1 shall apply. Areas with intact shop primer shall be sweep blasted to minimum lightly in accordance with SFS 8145, table 1, prior to application of the final coating system. All other areas shall be treated in accordance with the requirements for bare steel. \n\n4.8 Unpainted surfaces \n\nThe following items shall not be coated unless otherwise specified: \n\nx aluminium, titanium, uninsulated stainless steel, insulated stainless steel heating/ventilation/air-conditioning ducts, chrome plated, nickel plated, copper, brass, lead, plastic or similar; \n\nx jacketing materials on insulated surfaces. If stainless steel is connected to carbon steel, the stainless steel part shall be coated 50 mm beyond the weld zone onto the stainless steel. For piping and pressure vessels, the coating for the stainless steel part shall not contain metallic zinc .\n\n4.9 Handling and shipping of coated items \n\nCoated items shall be carefully handled to avoid damage to coated surfaces. No handling shall be performed before the coating system is cured to an acceptable level. Packing, handling and storage facilities shall be of non-metallic type. NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 7 of 20 \n\n4.10 Pre-qualification of products, personnel and procedures \n\nPre-qualification requirements as described in Clause 10 shall be fulfilled and documented prior to commencement of any work in accordance with this NORSOK standard. \n\n4.11 Metal coating \n\nHot-dip galvanising shall be in accordance with ISO 1461. Minimum coating thickness for structural items and outfitting steel shall be 125 \u03bcm and 900 g/m 2. Structural items shall be blast cleaned before hot-dip galvanising. When additional painting is specified, coating system no. 6 shall be used. Metal spraying shall be in accordance with the requirements in this NORSOK standard. \n\n4.12 Records and reports \n\nAll measurements and inspection results required by this NORSOK standard shall be recorded, and the records shall provide traceability to the associated parts of the coated objects. Contractor shall prepare and maintain reports that include these records. Defective work shall be reported and included in the records. \n\n## 5 Health, safety and environment \n\nThe following documentation shall be provided and used when evaluating coating systems: \n\nx chemical name of organic solvent, OAR number according to Norwegian regulations, and VOC content in g/l; \n\nx percentage of low molecular epoxy (molecular weight < 700); \n\nx content of hazardous substances, see NORSOK S-002; \n\nx specification of hazardous thermal degradation components ;\n\nx combustibility; \n\nx special handling precautions and personal protection. All coating products shall as a minimum be in accordance with relevant Norwegian regulatory requirements regardless of where the coating operation takes place. Content of quartz and heavy metals in blast cleaning media (see ISO 8504-2) shall be given. \n\n## 6 Surface preparation 6.1 Pre-blasting preparations \n\nSharp edges, fillets, corners and welds shall be rounded or smoothened by grinding (minimum radius 2 mm). Hard surface layers (e.g. resulting from flame cutting) shall be removed by grinding prior to blast cleaning. The surfaces shall be free from any foreign matter such as weld flux, residue, slivers, oil, grease, salt etc. prior to blast cleaning. All surfaces should be washed with clean fresh water prior to blast cleaning. Any oil and grease contamination shall be removed in accordance with SSPC/SSPM Volume 2, grade SP1, prior to blasting operations. Any major surface defects, particularly surface laminations or scabs detrimental to the protective coating system shall be removed by suitable dressing. Where such defects have been revealed during blast cleaning, and dressing has been performed, the dressed area shall be re-blasted to the specified standard. All welds shall be inspected and if necessary repaired prior to final blast cleaning of the area. Surface pores, cavities etc. shall be removed by suitable dressing or weld repair. \n\n6.2 Blast cleaning \n\nBlasting abrasives shall be dry, clean and free from contaminants, which will be detrimental to the performance of the coating. Size of abrasive particles for blast cleaning shall be such that the prepared surface profile height (anchor pattern profile) is in accordance with the requirements for the applicable coating system. The surface profile NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 8 of 20 shall be graded in accordance with ISO 8503. Grit shall be used as blast medium. The grit shall be non-metallic and free from chlorides when used on stainless steel substrates. The cleanliness of the blast cleaned surface shall be as referred to for each coating system in accordance with ISO 8501-1. \n\n6.3 Final surface condition \n\nThe surface to be coated shall be clean, dry, free from oil/grease, and have the specified roughness and cleanliness until the first coat is applied. Dust, blast abrasives etc. shall be removed from the surface after blast cleaning such that the particle quantity and particle size do not exceed rating 2 of ISO 8502-3. The maximum content of soluble impurities on the blasted surface as sampled using ISO 8502-6 and distilled water, shall not exceed a conductivity measured in accordance with ISO 8502-9 corresponding to a NaCl content of 20 mg/m 2.\n\n## 7 Paint application 7.1 General \n\nContrasting colours shall be used for each coat of paint. Hiding power of topcoat for specified colours shall be tested in accordance with ISO 2814. Contrast ratio shall not be less than 94 % at the specified topcoat thickness. The coating manufacturer shall provide a CSDS for each coating system to be used, containing at least the following information for each product: \n\nx surface pre-treatment requirements; \n\nx film thickness (maximum, minimum and specified); \n\nx maximum and minimum re-coating intervals at relevant temperatures; \n\nx information on thinners to be used (quantities and type); \n\nx coating repair system (qualified in accordance with Table 1). \n\n7.2 Application equipment \n\nThe method of application shall be governed by the coating manufacturer's recommendation for the particular coating being applied. Roller application of the first primer coat is not acceptable. When paints are applied by brush, the brush shall be of a style and quality acceptable to the coating manufacturer. Brush application shall be done so that a smooth coat, as uniform in thickness as possible, is obtained. \n\n7.3 Application \n\nFor each coat, a stripe coat shall be applied by brush to all welds, corners, behind angles, edges of beams etc. and areas not fully reachable by spray in order to obtain the specified coverage and thickness. Edges of existing coating shall be feathered towards the substrate prior to overcoating. Each coat shall be applied uniformly over the entire surface. Skips, runs, sags and drips shall be avoided. Each coat shall be free from pinholes, blisters and holidays. Contamination of painted surfaces between coats shall be avoided. Any contamination shall be removed. NORSOK standard M-501 Rev. 5, June 2004 NORSOK standard Page 9 of 20 \n\n## 8 Thermally sprayed metallic coatings 8.1 General \n\nRelevant requirements provided in this standard are applicable for thermally sprayed metallic coatings. Specific requirements valid for thermally sprayed metallic coatings are provided below. \n\n8.2 Coating materials \n\nThe materials for metal spraying shall be in accordance with the following: Aluminium: Type Al 99.5 of DIN 8566-2 or equivalent. Aluminium alloy: Aluminium alloy with 5 % Mg, DIN 8566-2 AlMg5 or equivalent. Zinc or alloys of zinc. All coating metals shall be supplied with product data sheets and quality control certificates, and be marked with coating metal manufacturer's name, manufacturing standard, metal composition, weight and manufacture date. Metal coating shall be sealed or overcoated as specified in coating system no. 2 in Annex A. Maximum operating temperature when zinc or alloys of zinc metal coating is used is 120 \u00b0C. \n\n8.3 Application of thermally sprayed coating \n\nEach coat shall be applied uniformly over the entire surface. The coat shall be applied in multiple layers and shall overlap on each pass of the gun. Equipment for application should follow guidelines given in DIN 32521. The coating shall be firmly adherent. The surface after spraying shall be uniform and free of lumps, loosely adherent spattered metal, bubbles, ash formation, defects and uncoated spots. Before application of any further coat, any damage to the previous coat shall be repaired. \n\n8.4 Repair, field coating of pipes and coating of in-fill steel \n\nAll requirements, including adhesion, applicable to metal spraying, shall apply. The treating and handling of the substrate shall be done in such a manner that the product in its final condition will have a continuous and uniform coating. Before the metal spraying operation starts, the area 30 cm to 40 cm in distance from the weld zone shall be sweep-blasted to ensure that all contamination is removed. The uncoated welding zone shall be blast cleaned as specified for coating system no. 2. The metal coating shall be performed according to 8.3. \n\n## 9 Sprayed on passive fire protection 9.1 General \n\nRelevant requirements provided in this NORSOK standard are applicable for sprayed on passive fire protection. Specific requirements valid for sprayed on passive fire protection are provided below. Information regarding selection of materials, thicknesse"

outputs\codex_findings\v28_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:3025:10. **Multi-source citation (M-27, for DR-grade citation density)**: When MULTIPLE evidence rows independently support the same claim, cite ALL of them, not just one. Example: "Tirzepatide reduced HbA1c 2.0-2.4% vs placebo across phase 3 trials [ev_012][ev_034][ev_055][ev_088]." rather than citing one ev_id. This raises citation density from ~1 per sentence to 2-4 per sentence where evidence supports it — top-tier DR (GPT-5.4 DR / Gemini 3.1 Pro DR) routinely reaches 50-200 citations for a clinical question by synthesizing multiple converging sources into each sentence, not by writing more sentences.

outputs\codex_findings\v27_deep_content_audit\_codex_stdout.txt:3078:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2\PG_LB_FS_01_health.json:315:      "content": "Landmark Regulatory Approvals in 2025\nSemaglutide (Ozempic) — Kidney Disease Indication\nOn January 28, 2025, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) expanded the label of semaglutide (Ozempic®) to include a chronic kidney disease (CKD) protection indication in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) and CKD (U.S. Food and Drug Administration [FDA], 2025a). This represented the first approval of a GLP-1 receptor agonist for renal outcomes, establishing a new therapeutic benchmark.The approval was based on the pivotal FLOW trial (NCT03819153), a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled outcomes study enrolling 3,533 participants across 28 countries. Results showed a 24% reduction in the composite primary endpoint of sustained ≥ 50% eGFR decline, end-stage kidney disease, renal death, or cardiovascular death (HR 0.76; 95% CI, 0.66–0.88; p = 0.0003) (Perkovic & Tuttle, 2024). Secondary analyses confirmed consistent benefits across major outcomes: kidney-specific composite (HR 0.79; 95% CI, 0.66–0.94), cardiovascular death (HR 0.71; 95% CI, 0.56–0.89), major adverse cardiovascular events (HR 0.82; 95% CI, 0.68–0.98), and all-cause mortality (HR 0.80; 95% CI, 0.67–0.95). The mean annual eGFR decline was attenuated by 1.16 mL/min/1.73m² in semaglutide recipients compared with placebo (FLOW Trial Investigators, 2024). These findings are summarized graphically in Fig. 2, which demonstrates hazard ratios and confidence intervals for the key endpoints.19,20\nForest plot showing hazard ratios (95% CI) for the composite kidney + cardiovascular endpoint and major secondary outcomes from the FLOW trial (Perkovic & Tuttle, 2024; FDA, 2025a).\nThe regulatory impact was global. In December 2024, the European Medicines Agency (EMA)’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) adopted a positive opinion for semaglutide’s kidney indication (European Medicines Agency [EMA], 2024). Subsequently, the Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) of Australia approved the indication in August 2025 (Therapeutic Goods Administration [TGA], 2025). Collectively, these decisions illustrate growing harmonization of regulatory approaches in addressing the multi-organ burden of diabetes. The clinical implications are profound: semaglutide now provides validated renal and cardiovascular protection in addition to glycemic and weight benefits.21–24 This expands treatment beyond glucose control to a comprehensive cardio-renal-metabolic approach, shifting practice guidelines across endocrinology, nephrology, and cardiology (American Diabetes Association [ADA], 2025). The approval of semaglutide is contextualized within a broader wave of 2025 antidiabetic drug approvals (Table 1).\nTable 1\nSummary of 2025 Novel Antidiabetic Drug Approvals\n|\nDrug (Brand)\n|\nClass / Mechanism\n|\nSponsor\n|\nRegulatory Agency (Region)\n|\nApproval / Action Date (2025)\n|\nIndication / Use\n|\nKey Trial / Evidence\n|\nPrimary Endpoint & Effect Size\n|\nDosage / Presentation\n|\nNotes\n|\n|\nSemaglutide (Ozempic)\n|\nGLP-1 receptor agonist\n|\nNovo Nordisk\n|\nFDA (US); TGA (AU); EMA CHMP positive opinion (EU)\n|\nJan 28 (FDA); Aug 20 (TGA label expansion)\n|\nReduce risk of worsening kidney disease, kidney failure, and CV death in adults with T2D + CKD\n|\nFLOW (NCT03819153)\n|\nComposite kidney/CV death HR 0.76 (95% CI, 0.66–0.88)\n|\nOnce-weekly 1.0 mg s.c. pen (Ozempic presentations)\n|\nFirst GLP-1 RA with renal protection indication\n|\n|\nMerilog (insulin aspart-szjj)\n|\nRapid-acting insulin biosimilar to NovoLog\n|\nSanofi-Aventis U.S. LLC\n|\nFDA (US)\n|\nFeb 14\n|\nImprove glycemic control in adults and pediatric patients with diabetes mellitus\n|\nBiosimilarity analytical/PK-PD + clinical confirmatory\n|\nNo clinically meaningful differences vs reference; mealtime glucose control\n|\n100 U/mL; 3 mL prefilled pen (SoloStar) & 10 mL vial\n|\nFirst rapid-acting insulin biosimilar approved in US\n|\n|\nKirsty (insulin aspart-xjhz)\n|\nRapid-acting insulin biosimilar (interchangeable) to NovoLog\n|\nBiocon Biologics\n|\nFDA (US)\n|\nJul 15\n|\nImprove glycemic control in adults and pediatric patients with diabetes mellitus\n|\nSwitching study + totality-of-evidence for interchangeability\n|\nMeets statutory standards for interchangeability (automatic substitution)\n|\n100 U/mL; prefilled pen (s.c.) & multi-dose vial (s.c./i.v.)\n|\nFirst interchangeable rapid-acting insulin in US\n|\n|\nPrusogliptin (Shan Ze Ping)\n|\nDPP-4 inhibitor\n|\nCSPC Ouyi Pharmaceutical\n|\nNMPA (China)\n|\nJan 8\n|\nType 2 diabetes in adults\n|\nPhase III dossier (China)\n|\nHbA1c reduction vs comparator/placebo (per dossier)\n|\nOral tablets\n|\nCategory 1 innovative drug in China\n|\n|\nCofrogliptin (倍长平)\n|\nDPP-4 inhibitor\n|\nHaisco Pharmaceutical Group\n|\nNMPA (China)\n|\nFeb 19\n|\nImprove blood glucose control in adults with T2D\n|\nPhase III dossier (China)\n|\nHbA1c reduction vs comparator/placebo (per dossier)\n|\nOral tablets\n|\nCategory 1 innovative drug in China\n|\n|\nInsulin icodec (Awiqli)\n|\nOnce-weekly basal insulin analog\n|\nNovo Nordisk\n|\nNMPA (China)\n|\nFeb 19 (public English site update)\n|\nAdults with type 2 diabetes (China)\n|\nONWARDS Phase 3 program\n|\nHbA1c reduction non-inferior/superior vs daily basal insulins depending on trial\n|\n100 U/mL once-weekly pen\n|\nFirst once-weekly basal insulin marketed globally (ex-US)\n|\n|\nTirzepatide (Mounjaro KwikPen)\n|\nDual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist\n|\nEli Lilly and Company\n|\nCDSCO (India)\n|\nJun (availability announced Aug 2025)\n|\nAdjunct to diet/exercise for T2D; obesity indication per local label\n|\nSURPASS program\n|\nHbA1c and body weight reduction vs comparators\n|\nOnce-weekly KwikPen in multiple strengths\n|\nFirst dual GIP/GLP-1 widely available in India\n|\n|\nMazdutide\n|\nDual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist\n|\nInnovent Biologics (licensed from Eli Lilly)\n|\nNMPA (China)\n|\nJun 27\n|\nChronic weight management (overweight/obesity, adults)\n|\nDREAMS & GLORY programs; Phase 3 vs dulaglutide (T2D)\n|\nWeight loss and HbA1c superiority vs placebo / dulaglutide (per trials)\n|\nAutoinjector; various mg strengths\n|\nWorld’s first approved dual GLP-1/glucagon agonist\n|\nTable 1 provides a consolidated overview of novel antidiabetic drug approvals during the year 2025, across multiple therapeutic classes and global regulatory regions. Abbreviations: FDA = U.S. Food and Drug Administration; TGA = Therapeutic Goods Administration (Australia); EMA = European Medicines Agency; NMPA = National Medical Products Administration (China); CDSCO = Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (India); GLP-1 RA = glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist; GIP = glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide; DPP-4 = dipeptidyl peptidase-4; CKD = chronic kidney disease; CV = cardiovascular; HR = hazard ratio; HbA1c = glycated hemoglobin.\nPrimary endpoints and effect sizes were extracted from pivotal trials and regulatory documentation. For semaglutide (Ozempic®), data are derived from the FLOW trial (NCT03819153), which demonstrated a 24% relative risk reduction in composite kidney and cardiovascular outcomes (Perkovic & Tuttle, 2024; FDA, 2025a). Insulin biosimilar approvals (Merilog® and Kirsty®) were based on comprehensive biosimilarity assessments and switching studies meeting FDA criteria for interchangeability (FDA, 2025b; FDA, 2025c). Approvals of prusogliptin and cofrogliptin in China represent Category 1 innovative drugs developed under expedited pathways (NMPA, 2025a; 2025b). Insulin icodec (Awiqli®) marked the first once-weekly basal insulin, supported by the ONWARDS clinical program (Novo Nordisk, 2025). Tirzepatide (Mounjaro®) and mazdutide approvals highlight the expansion of dual-acting incretin therapies, with tirzepatide being the first dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist in India, and mazdutide the first globally approved dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist (CDSCO, 2025; Innovent Biologics, 2025).\nThe inclusion of sponsor, dosage forms, and regional variations underscores the heterogeneity of global access and the dual trends of innovation (GLP-1 RAs, dual agonists, once-weekly insulins) and affordability (biosimilars). While data presented reflect the most recent regulatory communications and peer-reviewed trial results, post-marketing surveillance and real-world evidence will be essential for confirming long-term efficacy and safety across diverse patient populations.\nInsulin Biosimilars\nA landmark in 2025 diabetes care was the approval of two major insulin biosimilars in the United States, addressing the urgent issue of insulin affordability. For decades, insulin pricing has remained a global controversy, with millions of patients struggling to afford essential therapy. In the United States alone, approximately 8.4 million patients rely on insulin, many of whom experience high out-of-pocket costs (American Diabetes Association [ADA], 2025). Against this backdrop, the FDA’s approval of Merilog® in February 2025 and Kirsty® in July 2025 marked critical advances in patient access.25\nMerilog (Insulin Aspart-szjj): First Rapid-Acting Biosimilar\nOn February 14, 2025, the U.S. FDA approved Merilog® (insulin aspart-szjj), manufactured by Sanofi-Aventis, as the first rapid-acting insulin biosimilar in the U.S. market (FDA, 2025b). Merilog is biosimilar to Novo Nordisk’s NovoLog® (insulin aspart) and is indicated for improving glycemic control in both adults and pediatric patients with diabetes mellitus.26,27 Approval was based on a totality-of-evidence approach, which included extensive analytical, nonclinical, and clinical confirmatory data. The FDA concluded that there were no clinically meaningful differences between Merilog and NovoLog in terms of purity, potency, safety, or efficacy (FDA, 2025b). Importantly, Merilog was launched in both 10 mL multiple-dose vials and 3 mL SoloStar prefilled pens, offering flexibility in clinical practice (Sanofi, 2025).\nKirsty (Insulin Aspart-xjhz): First Interchangeable Rapid-Acting Insulin\nThe FDA expanded this biosimilar landscape further on July 15, 2025, when it approved Kirsty® (insulin aspart-xjhz), developed by Biocon Biologics, as the first interchangeable rapid-acting insulin aspart biosimilar (FDA, 2025c). This designation is highly significant: under U.S. law, an “interchangeable” biosimilar can be substituted at the pharmacy level without prescriber intervention, subject to state pharmacy laws. The designation was supported by results from a randomized phase 3 switching study (NCT03760068) involving 478 patients with type 1 diabetes over 24 weeks, comparing insulin aspart-xjhz to NovoLog®. Findings showed no clinically meaningful differences in efficacy, immunogenicity, or safety between the biosimilar and its reference product (Biocon Biologics, 2025).\nClinical and Market Implications\nThe introduction of Merilog and Kirsty represents a major step forward in affordability. Insulin biosimilars are projected to reduce treatment costs by 21–79% depending on market dynamics, potentially saving billions in healthcare expenditures over the next decade (Research and Markets, 2025). By expanding treatment accessibility, biosimilars are expected to improve adherence and glycemic control, thereby lowering the risk of long-term diabetes complications.In the broader context of 2025, these approvals align with global trends emphasizing sustainability in diabetes care—balancing innovation (e.g., semaglutide renal indication, dual agonists) with affordability through biosimilars (see Table 1). The regulatory milestones of Merilog and Kirsty are visually situated in the 2025 drug approval timeline (Fig. 3).\nThis figure depicts the chronological sequence of landmark regulatory approvals for novel antidiabetic therapies in 2025. Approvals in the United States (FDA, 💊) included semaglutide (Ozempic®) with a chronic kidney disease indication (January 28), Merilog® (insulin aspart-szjj), the first rapid-acting insulin biosimilar (February 14), and Kirsty® (insulin aspart-xjhz), the first interchangeable rapid-acting insulin biosimilar (July 15). In China (NMPA, 🌏), prusogliptin (January 8) and cofrogliptin (February 19) were approved as novel DPP-4 inhibitors, followed by insulin icodec (Awiqli®), the first once-weekly basal insulin (February 19), and mazdutide, the world’s first dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist (June 27). In India (CDSCO, 🇮🇳), tirzepatide (Mounjaro®) received approval on June 26 as the first dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist available in that market. Finally, in Australia (TGA, 🇦🇺), semaglutide’s CKD indication was expanded in August 2025. Together, these regulatory milestones highlight 2025 as a watershed year for diabetes therapeutics, integrating innovations in multi-organ protection, biosimilar affordability, and dual-acting incretin strategies.28\nNovel DPP-4 Inhibitors (China)\nPrusogliptin (Shan Ze Ping, CSPC Ouyi)\nIn January 2025, the National Medical Products Administration (NMPA) approved prusogliptin (善泽平, Shan Ze Ping), developed by CSPC Ouyi, as a Category 1 innovative DPP-4 inhibitor (CSPC Pharmaceutical Group, 2025; NMPA, 2025a). In pivotal phase 3 studies, once-daily prusogliptin 100 mg demonstrated significant HbA1c reduction (− 0.61% vs placebo at 24 weeks) and was non-inferior to sitagliptin 100 mg, with efficacy sustained through 52 weeks (Wang et al., 2024). When added to metformin, prusogliptin achieved a placebo-adjusted HbA1c reduction of − 0.63%, with neutral effects on body weight and minimal hypoglycemia risk (Xu et al., 2022). Pharmacokinetic analyses confirmed stable inhibition of DPP-4 activity, and no dose adjustments were required in patients with mild to moderate renal impairment (Liu et al., 2025).\nCofrogliptin (Bei Chang Ping, Haisco)\nOn February 19, 2025, the NMPA approved cofrogliptin (倍长平, Bei Chang Ping), developed by Haisco Pharmaceutical, as another Category 1 innovative drug (NMPA, 2025b). Cofrogliptin is the world’s first ultra-long-acting oral DPP-4 inhibitor, with a dosing interval of once every two weeks (Q2W). In a large phase 3 non-inferiority trial, cofrogliptin 10 mg or 25 mg Q2W achieved HbA1c reductions of − 0.6% to − 1.0% and was non-inferior to linagliptin 5 mg QD; benefits were maintained during a 28-week open-label extension (Ren et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2025). This extended-interval dosing model, informed by model-informed drug development (MIDD) approaches, reduces pill burden and has the potential to improve long-term adherence in real-world practice (Gao et al., 2024).29\nMechanistic Rationale and Class Effects\nBoth prusogliptin and cofrogliptin exert their effect by inhibiting dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4), thereby increasing circulating levels of GLP-1 and GIP. This mechanism enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion from β-cells, suppresses glucagon secretion from α-cells, reduces hepatic glucose production, and slows gastric emptying (Florentin et al., 2022; Ahrén, 2019). The result is improved glycemic control with weight neutrality and a low intrinsic risk of hypoglycemia, especially when not combined with sulfonylureas or insulin. These mechanistic and clinical features are illustrated in Fig. 4.\nFigure 4. Mechanism of DPP-4 inhibition and clinical outcomes. By preventing incretin degradation, prusogliptin and cofrogliptin enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion, reduce glucagon, slow gastric emptying, and lower hepatic glucose output, leading to improved HbA1c with weight neutrality and low hypoglycemia risk.30\nComparative Clinical and Regulatory Profiles\nWhile both agents belong to the same class, their clinical positioning diverges. Prusogliptin represents a daily oral DPP-4 inhibitor with a profile similar to existing class members, but with the distinction of being locally innovated in China. In contrast, cofrogliptin’s biweekly dosing represents a global first, targeting adherence and treatment convenience. Their complementary characteristics highlight the dual direction of China’s antidiabetic drug strategy in 2025: incremental clinical innovation and pharmacokinetic breakthroughs. This contrast is summarized in Table 2 and illustrated in Figure 5.\nTable 2. Comparative profile of novel DPP-4 inhibitors approved in China (2025)\n|\nAttribute\n|\nPrusogliptin (DBPR108, 善泽平)\n|\nCofrogliptin (HSK7653, 倍长平)\n|\n|\nSponsor/Developer\n|\nCSPC Ouyi Pharmaceutical\n|\nHaisco Pharmaceutical Group\n|\n|\nRegulatory pathway / status\n|\nNMPA approval, Category 1 innovative drug(Jan 2025)\n|\nNMPA approval, Category 1 innovative drug(Feb 2025)\n|\n|\nOfficial approval communication\n|\nSponsor announcement Jan 13, 2025; Gao et al., 2025)\n|\n|\nAdd-on evidence\n|\nPlacebo-adjusted HbA1c −0.63% at 24 wks as add-on to metformin; no weight gain (Xu et al., 2022)\n|\nMaintained HbA1c reductions during 28-wk open-label extension with 25 mg Q2W (Gao et al., 2025)\n|\n|\nWeight / hypoglycemia profile\n|\nWeight-neutral; dosing per final Chinese label\n|\n|\nMechanistic/PK highlight\n|\nStable once-daily DPP-4 inhibition confirmed in PK/PD studies (Liu et al., 2025)\n|\nUltralong-acting inhibitor; MIDD modeling supported extended Q2W dosing (Gao et al., 2024)\n|\nNMPA = National Medical Products Administration; MIDD = model-informed drug development. Table 2 compares the two Category 1 innovative DPP-4 inhibitors approved in China in 2025. Prusogliptin (Shan Ze Ping, CSPC) was approved in January 2025 following phase 3 trials demonstrating non-inferiority to sitagliptin and significant HbA1c reduction with low risk of hypoglycemia (Wang et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2022). Cofrogliptin (Bei Chang Ping, Haisco) was approved in February 2025 as the world’s first ultra-long-acting, Q2W DPP-4 inhibitor, confirmed in non-inferiority trials against daily linagliptin with durable glycemic benefits (Ren et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2024, 2025). Both agents maintain the class advantages of weight neutrality and glucose-dependent action, minimizing hypoglycemia risk (Florentin et al., 2022; Ahrén, 2019).\nAdvanced Insulin Formulations\nInsulin Icodec (Awiqli) — A Once-Weekly Basal Insulin\nIn February 2025, the National Medical Products Administration (NMPA) approved insulin icodec injection (Awiqli®, 诺和期), developed by Novo Nordisk, as the world’s first once-weekly basal insulin. Unlike daily basal insulins (e.g., insulin glargine, insulin degludec), insulin icodec is engineered with a C20 fatty diacid side chain that binds strongly but reversibly to serum albumin, forming a circulating depot for sustained, slow release over seven days. This pharmacological profile provides flat, stable insulin levels, reducing variability and supporting effective glycemic control with just one injection per week.31\nClinical Evidence — ONWARDS Phase 3 Program\nThe ONWARDS clinical program, comprising six pivotal trials, evaluated insulin icodec in insulin-naïve and insulin-experienced patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) as well as individuals with type 1 diabetes (T1D).\n-\nIn insulin-naïve T2D patients, once-weekly icodec achieved HbA1c reductions of ~ 1.3%, non-inferior to daily insulin degludec, with no increase in severe hypoglycemia (ONWARDS 1, 2).\n-\nIn insulin-experienced T2D patients, icodec maintained glycemic control comparable to glargine U100, with high patient satisfaction (ONWARDS 3, 4).\n-\nIn T1D, ONWARDS 6 confirmed non-inferiority but emphasized the need for careful titration and education to minimize hypoglycemia risk.\nCollectively, the ONWARDS data demonstrated that weekly dosing did not compromise safety or efficacy, with treatment satisfaction significantly higher among patients randomized to icodec.\nAdherence and Patient-Centered Benefits\nThe once-weekly dosing reduces the injection burden from 365 to 52 injections per year, directly addressing patient concerns of treatment fatigue, complexity, and injection anxiety. Early patient-reported outcomes highlight greater confidence, less distress, and improved quality of life with icodec compared to daily insulins. This innovation may help overcome therapeutic inertia, a major barrier to timely insulin initiation. Furthermore, by lowering injection frequency, insulin icodec has the potential to improve adherence in low- and middle-income settings, where irregular dosing and supply chain issues often compromise glycemic control.\nClinical and Regulatory Significance\nThe 2025 approval of insulin icodec represents a transformative advancement in insulin therapy. By combining a novel albumin-binding mechanism with proven glycemic efficacy and favorable safety, insulin icodec has the potential to reshape the landscape of basal insulin therapy, simplify patient care, and improve long-term outcomes globally.\nTable 3\nClinical and regulatory profile of insulin icodec (Awiqli®) compared with traditional basal insulins\n|\nAttribute\n|\nInsulin Icodec (Awiqli®, Novo Nordisk)\n|\nDaily Basal Insulins (Glargine, Degludec)\n|\n|\nRegulatory milestone\n|\nApproved by NMPA (China), Feb 2025; under review by EMA, FDA, TGA\n|\nApproved globally over 2000s–2010s\n|\n|\nMolecular mechanism\n|\nInsulin analog with C20 fatty diacid side chain → reversible albumin binding → slow continuous release\n|\nModified amino acid sequences; protraction via zinc or fatty acid chains → once-daily action\n|\n|\nDosing frequency\n|\nOnce weekly (QW)\n|\nOnce daily (QD)\n|\n|\nONWARDS trial HbA1c effect\n|\nHbA1c reduction ~ 1.3% in T2D (non-inferior to degludec, glargine)\n|\nHbA1c reduction ~ 1.2–1.4% depending on regimen\n|\n|\nHypoglycemia risk\n|\nComparable to daily insulins in T2D; positions Novo Nordisk as leader in basal insulin innovation\n|\nStandard of care for two decades\n|\nNMPA = National Medical Products Administration; EMA = European Medicines Agency; FDA = U.S. Food and Drug Administration; TGA = Therapeutic Goods Administration; QD = once daily; QW = once weekly; T2D = type 2 diabetes; T1D = type 1 diabetes.\nDual and Novel Mechanism Drugs\nTirzepatide: Dual GIP/GLP-1 Receptor Agonist (India, June 2025)\nIn June 2025, the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) of India approved tirzepatide (Mounjaro®, Eli Lilly), expanding access to the world’s first dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist in this market (Reuters, 2025; Business Standard, 2025; BioSpectrum India, 2025). The KwikPen formulation was authorized for once-weekly subcutaneous administration across titration doses ranging from 2.5 mg to 15 mg. The efficacy of tirzepatide has been established through the SURPASS phase 3 program. In the pivotal SURPASS J-mono trial, conducted in Japanese patients with type 2 diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior glycemic efficacy and body weight reduction compared with dulaglutide 0.75 mg, which is the standard comparator dose in Japan (Kadowaki et al., 2022). At 52 weeks, tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg achieved mean HbA1c reductions of − 2.37% to − 2.55%, compared with − 1.30% for dulaglutide, alongside dose-dependent weight loss (Kadowaki et al., 2022). Patient-reported outcomes confirmed higher treatment satisfaction with tirzepatide relative to dulaglutide (Terauchi et al., 2022). Furthermore, the recently completed SURPASS-CVOT trial (2025) demonstrated that tirzepatide was non-inferior to dulaglutide for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)"
