positions Novo Nordisk as leader in basal insulin innovation\n|\nStandard of care for two decades\n|\nNMPA = National Medical Products Administration; EMA = European Medicines Agency; FDA = U.S. Food and Drug Administration; TGA = Therapeutic Goods Administration; QD = once daily; QW = once weekly; T2D = type 2 diabetes; T1D = type 1 diabetes.\nDual and Novel Mechanism Drugs\nTirzepatide: Dual GIP/GLP-1 Receptor Agonist (India, June 2025)\nIn June 2025, the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) of India approved tirzepatide (Mounjaro®, Eli Lilly), expanding access to the world’s first dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist in this market (Reuters, 2025; Business Standard, 2025; BioSpectrum India, 2025). The KwikPen formulation was authorized for once-weekly subcutaneous administration across titration doses ranging from 2.5 mg to 15 mg. The efficacy of tirzepatide has been established through the SURPASS phase 3 program. In the pivotal SURPASS J-mono trial, conducted in Japanese patients with type 2 diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior glycemic efficacy and body weight reduction compared with dulaglutide 0.75 mg, which is the standard comparator dose in Japan (Kadowaki et al., 2022). At 52 weeks, tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg achieved mean HbA1c reductions of − 2.37% to − 2.55%, compared with − 1.30% for dulaglutide, alongside dose-dependent weight loss (Kadowaki et al., 2022). Patient-reported outcomes confirmed higher treatment satisfaction with tirzepatide relative to dulaglutide (Terauchi et al., 2022). Furthermore, the recently completed SURPASS-CVOT trial (2025) demonstrated that tirzepatide was non-inferior to dulaglutide for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)"

Analysis of the Effect of Different Surface Preparation Methods on Corrosion Analysis of the Effect of Different Surface Preparation Methods on Corrosion \n\nResistance and Adhesion Strength of ASTM A36 Steel Substrate with Surface Resistance and Adhesion Strength of ASTM A36 Steel Substrate with Surface \n\nTolerant Epoxy Paint as Coating Material Tolerant Epoxy Paint as Coating Material \n\nCover Page Footnote Cover Page Footnote \n\nThe authors would like to express our sincere gratitude to the Department of Metallurgical and Materials \n\nEngineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universitas Indonesia who has supported and guided this research.        \n\n> This article is available in Journal of Materials Exploration and Findings: https://scholarhub.ui.ac.id/jmef/vol3/iss1/2 Journal of Materials Exploration and Findings 3(1) 16 -29 (2024)\n> Received February 2024/Revised May 2024/ Accepted May 2024\n\nJOURNAL OF MATERIALS EXPLORATION AND FINDINGS \n\n> https://scholarhub.ui.ac.id/jmef/\n\n## Analysis of the Effect of Different Surface Preparation Methods on Corrosion \n\n## Resistance and Adhesion Strength of ASTM A36 Steel Substrate with Surface \n\n## Tolerant Epoxy Paint as Coating Material \n\nIrwan W ijaya Santoso 1,a , Daffa Aqila 1,b , Rini Riastuti 1 and Rizal T resna Ramadhani 1    \n\n> 1Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universitas Indonesia, Depok,\n> 16424, Indonesia\n> Corresponding author\u2019s email: airwan.wijaya@ui.ac.id ;bdaffa.aqi la@ui.ac.id\n\nAbstract. In the industrial world, to extend the service life of materials, protection methods are carried \n\nout to slow down the material's corrosion rate. The protection method that is often used is the coating \n\nmethod. The coating method is a protection method by co ating the substrate material using a coating \n\nmaterial to prevent contact between the substrate material and the environment. In this research, the \n\nsubstrate material used is ASTM A36 steel and the coating material used is Surface Tolerant Epoxy paint. \n\nThe independent variable used in this study lies in the surface preparation method which consists of: \n\nsolvent cleaning, hand tool cleaning, power tool cleaning, power tool to bare metal cleaning, and abrasive \n\nblast cleaning. Different preparation methods resul t in different roughness and cleanliness of the surface. \n\nThis can affect changes in the mechanical properties of the coating material, such as corrosion resistance \n\nand adhesion strength. Based on the corrosion resistance test, it is found that the abrasive blast cleaning \n\nand power tool to bare metal cleaning methods produce the highest corrosion resistance properties \n\nbecause both have a rating number of 8 in the salt spray test results. Based on the adhesion strength \n\ntest, it is found that the abrasive blas t cleaning method also produces the highest adhesion strength. This \n\nconclusion refers to the results of the tape x -cut test where the sample produces a rating number 5A \n\nwhere the sample does not experience peeling after testing. In addition, the abrasive b last cleaning \n\nmethod produced the highest adhesion strength in the pull -off test, which was 7.16 MPa . Thus, the \n\nabrasive blast cleaning method is the most effective surface preparation method for ASTM A36 steel \n\nbefore being coated with the coating material. In addition, it can also be concluded that the higher the \n\nsurface roughness of the sample, the better the corrosion resistance and adhesion strength . Whereas \n\nabrasive blast cleaning method have 122.95 \u03bcm roughness and 7.16 MPa adhesion strength w hich is the \n\nhighest in roughness and adhesion strength .\n\nKeywords: Adhesion; Corrosion; Steel ASTM A36; Surface Preparation; Surface Tolerant Epoxy \n\n1. Introduction \n\nSteel is a material that is often chosen for various sectors, such as the automotive, \n\nconstruction, oil and gas, electronics, heavy equipment, transportation, defen ce equipment, \n\ntelecommunications, etc. It fulfils the needs for all construction processes and is often called as \n\nthe mother of Industry, which means the mother of al l industries (Nugraha and Mochtar, 2023; \n\nYuhandri et al. , 2023). 17  \n\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n\nBased on data obtained from Worldsteels, world steel demand is expected to continue to \n\nincrease in 2022 by 0.4% or reaching 1,840.2 million tons and in 2023 by 2.2% or reaching 1,881.4 \n\nmilli on tons (World Steel Association , 2022) . This is certainly good news for Indonesia, which has \n\niron ore reserves of 1.7% of total world reserves and iron ore production of 0.2% of total world \n\nproduction (Kementerian ESDM RI 2021) . In fact, based on data obtained from the Ministry of \n\nIndustry, it is said that Indonesia's iron and steel trade balance experienced benefits with an \n\nincrease in exports from 2020 to 2021 of 51.8% (KEMENPERIN RI , 2022) . Therefore, if Indonesia, \n\nwhich is rich in iron ore, is supported by mastery of advanced steel tech nology, it will certainly \n\nhelp this country become a developed country. \n\nIn its application, steel materials have great potential to interact with the natural \n\natmosphere. This is what makes steel susceptible to corrosion. Corrosion is considered \n\ndetrimental because parts affected by corrosion will experience decomposition, resulting in a \n\ndecrease in the quality of the steel and also making the steel prone to failure (Umeozokwere et \n\nal. , 2016) . Corrosion is something that cannot be avoided, but can be manag ed and controlled. \n\nBy treating and controlling corrosion, the service life of steel materials will be longer, losses due \n\nto failure can be minimized, and worker safety will be maintained. \n\nHence, to reduce the possibility of corrosion, steel materials need to be given a coating \n\ntreatment. Coating treatment is a method of coating the surface of a material using corrosion -\n\nresistant material, and thus the possibility of a reaction between the material and the \n\nenvironment can be minimized (Hasanah et al ., 2023) . One of the most frequently used coating \n\nmethods is the organic coating method. Organic coatings are considered to have effective \n\ncoating results in inhibiting corrosion, tend to be easy to apply, and also provide added value in \n\nthe form of aesthetic value. However, it should be noted that the quality of the coating produced \n\nis not only determined on the type of coating material used. There are other factors, such as: \n\nenvironmental conditions around the steel, surface preparation that has been carried out, \n\naccuracy of the operator in coating, etc. Among these factors, surface preparation is the factor \n\nthat has the most significant i mpa ct on the quality of the resulting layer. Sample preparation is \n\noften the main cause of coating failure. There are fail ures that often occur, such as: the presence \n\nof bubbles that are still trapped between the surface of the substrate and the coating layer, low \n\nadhesion to the layer so that it is easily degraded, shrinkage occurs, blistering phenomena, etc. \n\nThese things ca n happen due to weak adhesion properties between the substrate surface and \n\nthe layer, so that the layer cannot adhere optimally (Schoff , 1999) . The non -adhesion of the layer \n\nwas caused by the sample preparation treatment that had been carried out which w as deemed \n\ninappropriate. The sample preparation carried out did not comply with the Steel Surface Painting \n\nCouncil (SSPC) standards. Starting from the surface cleaning method, mechanical treatments, \n\nsurface roughness is not done properly so that the result ing adhesion strength is not optimal \n\n(Weldon , 2009) .\n\nTherefore, in this research various experiments were carried out in using coating methods \n\nwith surface tolerant epoxy paint on the surface of ASTM A36 steel substrates. This research \n\nuses a variety of surface preparations, including solvent cleaning, hand tool cleaning, power tool \n\ncleaning, abrasive blast cleaning, and power tool to bare metal cleaning. This report will also \n\ndiscuss the testing methods used and their objectives. The expected result of t his research is to \n\nfind out the effect of the surface preparation used on corrosion resistance and the adhesion \n\nstrength between the substrate material and the layer that protects the surface. 18  \n\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n\n2. Methods \n\n2.1. Surface Preparation \n\nThe sample material used in this research was ASTM A36 steel, with dimensions of 70 x 150 \n\nx 3 mm and was at rust grade B. In this research, the samples were divided into 5 groups based \n\non the type of sample preparation (solvent cleaning, hand tool cleaning , power tool cleaning, \n\nabrasive blast cleaning, power tool to bare metal cleaning). It can be seen on table 1. Solvent \n\nCleaning carried by using cloth and solvent agent such as thinner clean the surface . Hand tool \n\ncleaning carried by using wire brush, sandpaper grit #180 #80, and scraper. Power tool cleaning \n\ncarried by using grind with steel wire brush cup. Power tool to bare metal cleaning using grind \n\nand flap disc cup. Abrasive blast cleaning carried by using blast machine. \n\nTable 1 Coding sample based on preparation method             \n\n> No Preparation Method Code\n> 1Solvent Cleaning SC\n> 2Hand Tool Cleaning HTC\n> 3Power Tool Cleaning PTC\n> 4Power Tool to Bare Metal Cleaning PTBMC\n> 5Abrasive Blast Cleaning ABC\n\n2. 2. Preparation of Organic Coating Materials \n\nIn this research, the type of paint used was surface tolerant epoxy paint. In its application, \n\nthis type of paint was intended for components that could not be regularly maintained, such as: \n\npainting ships, infrast ructure, storage tanks, the oil and gas industry, and other industries. \n\nBefore applying, it needed to prepare the paint first by referring to the guidelines in the technical \n\ndata sheet for the related paint type in order to obtain appropriate results. Prep aration was by \n\npaying attention to the mixing ratio of each paint component. The paint component consisted \n\nof part A as base paint and part B as curing agent. The mixing ratio for this type of surface \n\ntolerant epoxy paint was 5.67 : 1. Apart from these two components, a solvent agent in the form \n\nof thinner was also added, amounting to 10% of the total components. Solution thinner was used \n\nto break down the viscosity of the paint and also make the application process easier. Then these \n\nthree compone nts were mixed using a paint mixer. \n\n2. 3. Organic Coating Application Process \n\nVisual inspection was carried out by co mpa ring the prepared samples with existing visual \n\nstandards. This aimed to ensure that the cleanliness level of the sample had met standar ds \n\nbefore finally being coated with organic coating material . Apart from preparing the coating \n\nmaterial and sample, it was also important to pay attention to the environmental conditions of \n\nthe painting area. The things to pay attention to when checking th e environmental conditions \n\naround the sample (environment test) were steel temperature, wet temperature, dry \n\ntemperature, dew point (DP), and relative humidity (RH). When the sample had met the desired \n\nlevel of surface cleanliness and the sample environmen t had also met the provisions of th e\n\nenvironment test. Then the sample was ready to be applied with organic coating material. The \n\napplication method used was the rolling method using a roller as the application tool .19  \n\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n\n2. 4. Surface Roughness \n\nThe method used for this research was a surface profile depth micrometer because it was \n\nconsidered to have the most accurate results and was easy to apply. The surface roughness \n\ntesting method using ASTM D4417 aimed to measure the depth of the sample surface profile. \n\nMeasurements were carried out using an Elcometer 224 Digital Surface Profile. Before using this \n\nmeasuring instrument, it needed to be calibrated first on a flat surface .\n\n2.5. Layer Thickness \n\nThe measurement process was accomplished twice, namely when the paint was still wet \n\n(wet film thickness) and when the paint was dry (dry film thickness). When the paint was still \n\nwet, the layer thickness test was done using an Elcometer 112 wet film thickness. When the \n\nsolvent contained in the paint had evapor ated and the paint was in a dry condition, the layer \n\nthickness test was made again using the PosiTector 6000 Coating Thickness Gauge. \n\nMeasurements were carried out at 5 different points on the sample and at each point 3 \n\nmeasurements were taken. Then, calcu lated the average of the 3 values from each point and the \n\ncombined average of the 5 points to get the overall dry film thickness (DFT) value .\n\n2.6. Characterization of Coating Corrosion Resistance and Coating Adhesion Strength Test \n\nThe tests implemented t o test the corrosion resistance of the organic coating layer were \n\nElectrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and salt spray test. The solution used to test the \n\ncorrosion resistance of the organic coating layer was NaCl 3.5% for the EIS and NaCl 5% for th e\n\nsalt spray test, refers to ASTM G106 -89 and ASTM B117 which were the standards for EIS and salt \n\nspray test .\n\nThe tests utilized to test the adhesion strength of the organic coating layer were tape test \n\nx-cut and pull off test. The tape test x -cut was a test characterization method which aimed to \n\ndetermine the adhesion strength of the organic coating to the ASTM A36 steel substrate. \n\nCharacterization method was done based on the ASTM D3359 standard. Pull -off test was a test \n\nmethod that aimed to determine the adhesion strength of the organic coating to the ASTM A38 \n\nsteel substrate. It referred to ASTM D4541 standard. This test was using PosiTest AT -M Adhesion \n\nTester, Dolly, and Cyanoacrylate glue .\n\n3. Results and Discussion \n\n3.1. Steel Surface Roughness \n\nThe results of the surface roughness test are shown in Figure 1. Based on surface \n\nroughness testing referring to ASTM D4417, the abrasive blast cleaning method was the method \n\nthat produced the highest surface roughness with a roughness value of 122.95 \u03bcm, followed by \n\nthe power tool to bare metal cleaning, solvent cleaning, power tool cleaning and hand tool \n\ncleaning methods with a roughness value of 80.28; 69.03; 19.68; and 14.72 \u03bcm. \n\nIn general, the results obtained were in accordance with the literature whi ch stated that the \n\nlevel of surface roughness produced will be directly proportional to the level of surface \n\ncleanliness. This was caused by the friction force used in the surface preparation method to \n\nremove impurities attached to the surface also resulte d surface degradation at the same time. \n\nThis degradation contributed to making the surface rougher (Praveen et al ., 2023) .20  \n\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n\nFigure 1 Surface roughness result \n\n3.2 Wet Film Thickness \n\nReferring to the technical data sheet for surface tolerant epoxy paint, the desired Dry Film \n\nThickness (DFT) in this research was 200 \u03bcm. If this number was converted in equation (1), then \n\nthe value of the wet film thickness (WFT) was obtained .\n\n\ud835\udc4a\ud835\udc52\ud835\udc61 \ud835\udc39\ud835\udc56\ud835\udc59\ud835\udc5a \ud835\udc47 \u210e\ud835\udc56\ud835\udc50\ud835\udc58\ud835\udc5b\ud835\udc52\ud835\udc60\ud835\udc60 = \ud835\udc37\ud835\udc5f\ud835\udc66 \ud835\udc39\ud835\udc56\ud835\udc59\ud835\udc5a \ud835\udc47 \u210e\ud835\udc56\ud835\udc50\ud835\udc58\ud835\udc5b\ud835\udc52\ud835\udc60\ud835\udc60    \n\n> %\ud835\udc49\ud835\udc5c\ud835\udc59\ud835\udc62\ud835\udc5a\ud835\udc52 \ud835\udc46\ud835\udc5c\ud835\udc59\ud835\udc56\ud835\udc51\n\n(1) \n\nBased on the technical data sheet, the solid volume of surface tolerant epoxy paint was 82 \u00b1 3%. \n\nIf the DFT used was 200 \u03bcm. Thus , the WFT o btained would be 243.90 \u03bcm. \n\n3.3 Dry Film Thickness \n\nWhen the applied paint ha s dried and cured on the substrate surface completely. DFT \n\nmeasurements were carried out. The results of measuring the DFT are shown in Figure 2. The \n\ncause of the resulting varying thickness values was because the coating material application \n\nprocess used a rolling method with a roller as the application tool. Please note that the rolling \n\nmethod had many limitations, such as: it had a high probability of producing layers that form \n\nunevenly. Generally, to minimize this, the spray method was often chosen becau se it could \n\nproduce a coating layer thickness that tended to be even and uniform. However, because the \n\ntype of surface tolerant epoxy paint had a high viscosity level, as a result the spray method could \n\nnot be used (Dong et al ., 2023) .21  \n\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n\nFigure 2 DFT result \n\n3.4. Salt Spray Test \n\nThe surface of the sample that had been coated with coating material was scratched using \n\na cutter to form an X pattern with a scratch width of \u00b1 0.5 mm (Wang et al ., 2016) . The average \n\nresult was compared from the initial scratch width to obtain the value of the change in scratch \n\nwidth. The scratch width change values were grouped based on the ASTM D1654 standard which \n\nis presented in Table 2 which is a reference for determin ing the rating number for each sampl e\n\n(Howard et al ., 1999) . Meanwhile t he data regarding the value of changes in scratch width which \n\nha d been grouped based on rating number has been summarized in Table 3.\n\nThe higher the rating, the better the corrosion resistance (Gad et al ., 2023) . abrasive blast \n\ncleaning and power tool to bare metal cleaning had the best level of corrosion resistance at \n\nrating 8. Meanwhile, samples treated with surface cleaning, hand tool cleaning and power tool \n\ncleaning ha d a corrosion resistance level that is slig htly below rating 7. This was because PTBMC \n\nand ABC had high surface roughness, so the paint could adhere better and provide good \n\ncorrosion resistance (Kiosidou et al ., 2018) .\n\nTable 2 Scratch widening reference (ASTM D1654)                                                                \n\n> Representative Mean Creepage From Scribe\n> Milimetres Inches (Approximate) Rating Number\n> Zero 010\n> Over 0 to 0.50 to 1/64 9\n> Over 0.5to 1.01/64 to 1/32 8\n> Over 1.0to 2.01/32 to 1/16 7\n> Over 2.0to 3.01/16 to 1/8 6\n> Over 3.0to 5.01/8 to 3/16 5\n> Over 5.0to 7.03/16 to 1/4 4\n> Over 7.0to 10 .01/4 to 3/8 3\n> Over 10 .0to 13 .03/8 to 1/2 2\n> Over 13 .0to 16 .01/2 to 5/8 1\n> Over 16 .0to more 5/8 to more 022\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n\nTable 3 Salt spray test results                           \n\n> Preparation Method Scribe width\n> (mm)\n> Creepage Width\n> (mm)\n> Representative Mean\n> Creepage from Scribe (mm) Rating\n> SC 0.51.72 1.22 7\n> HTC 0.51.64 1.14 7\n> PTC 0.51.87 1.37 7\n> PTBMC 0.51.47 0.97 8\n> ABC 0.51.32 0.82 8\n\n3.5. EIS Test \n\nThis test used a tool in the form of a flat cell which had function as a medium for \n\nelectrochemical cells to enable measurements of electrochemical impedance in various \n\nelectrochemical systems (Lazanas and Prodromidis , 2023) . In Figure 3, the Nyquist plo t from each \n\nsample form ed a scatter plot. The Ny qui st plot was useful for determining the stability of a \n\nsystem because it show ed the location of the poles and zeros of the system in the complex \n\nplane. Bode plot, was a plot of magnitude and phase function transfer system as a function of \n\nfrequency. \n\nIn this study, we used Nyquist plot because it showed the location of the poles and zeros. \n\nThe threshold value of chi -squared (\u03c72) was: (1) if the value of \u03c7 2 < 10 -6 indicated that the fitting \n\nresults were very suitable, (2) if the value of 10 -6 < \u03c72 < 10 -5, it was quite reasonable, (3) if the value \n\nof 10 -5 < \u03c7 2 < 10 -4 then it could be included in the acceptance threshold, and (4) if the value of \u03c7 2 >\n\n10 -4 then it was a bad value and could not be used as data interpretation (Lazanas and \n\nProdromidis , 2023) . Bas ed on this, it can be seen that none of the chi -squared values for each \n\nsample met the threshold . In this way, we can be sure that there were errors occurring in the \n\nresulting data, so that the resulting data could be said to be invalid and unusable. Usual ly, the \n\nscatter plot formed on a Nyquist graph was caused by non -uniformity of the coating layer, such \n\nas: uneven coating layer thickness, defects in the substrate, or impurities still attached to the \n\nsubstrate surface (Varacalle et al ., 2006) . The non -uniformity of the layer c ould cause variations \n\nin the impedance response at different measurement points, resulting in a scatter plot being \n\nformed on the Nyquist graph. \n\nTo analyse the corrosion resistance of the coating layer in protecting ASTM A36 ste el \n\nsubstrates from corrosive environments, 2 testing methods were applied , namely the salt spray \n\nmethod and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In the salt spray test, the results \n\nshowed that the abrasive blast cleaning method and power tool to b are metal cleaning were the \n\nsurface preparation methods with the highest level of effectiveness in preventing corrosion with \n\nchanges in the width of the scratches formed by 0.82 and 0.97 \u03bcm. Both were included in rating \n\nnumber 8. Meanwhile, in the EIS test , a randomly arranged pattern (scatter plot) was produced. \n\nThis ha d an i mpa ct on the resulting chi -square value which was also high (Ikani et al ., 2024) . When \n\nthe chi -square value was large, it indicated that the discrepancy between the experimental data \n\nand the predicted data from the equivalent circuit model was also large, and hence it could be \n\nascertained that the possibility of errors occurring in the resulting data was very large (Dong et \n\nal ., 2023; Momber et al ., 2024) .23     \n\n> doi: https://doi.org/10.7454/jmef.v3i1.10 44\n> (a) (b) (c)\n> (d) (e)\n\nFigure 3 EIS result s of (a) SC; (b) HTC; (c) PTC; (d) PTBMC; and (e) ABC samples \n\n3.6. Tape X -Cut Test \n\nThe tape x -cut test results can be seen in Figure 4 with various surface preparation methods. \n\nThe results of the inspection were compared with the illustrative images and the rating number \n\ndescription in Table 4 from ASTM D3359. Based on Figure 4 and Table 4, it can be seen that \n\nsolvent casting is in 3A Classification, meanwhile hand tool cleaning was in 4A classification and \n\npower tool cleaning , power tool to bare metal cleaning, and abrasive blast cleaning were in 5A \n\nclassifica tion each. \n\nSamples with solvent cleaning treatment had the lowest adhesion, while samples with \n\nabrasive blast clea"

bradleyw@uow.edu.au\n2Health Innovations, Faculty of Science, Medicine and Health, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia\nFind articles by [Marijka Batterham](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Batterham%20M\"\\[Author\\])\n1,2,*, [Bradley Wakefield](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Wakefield%20B\"\\[Author\\])\n### Bradley Wakefield\n1Statistical Consulting Centre, National Institute for Applied Statistics Research Australia, School of Mathematics and Applied Statistics, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia; bradleyw@uow.edu.au\nFind articles by [Bradley Wakefield](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Wakefield%20B\"\\[Author\\])\n1\nEditor: Chengwen Sun\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n1Statistical Consulting Centre, National Institute for Applied Statistics Research Australia, School of Mathematics and Applied Statistics, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia; bradleyw@uow.edu.au\n2Health Innovations, Faculty of Science, Medicine and Health, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia\n*\nCorrespondence: marijka@uow.edu.au\n#### Roles\n**Chengwen Sun** : Academic Editor\nReceived 2024 Dec 21; Revised 2025 Jan 9; Accepted 2025 Jan 15; Collection date 2025 Jan.\n\u00a9 2025 by the authors.\nLicensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland. This article is an open access article distributed under the terms and conditions of the Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license (<https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/>).\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC11768033 PMID: [39861462](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39861462/)\n##  Abstract\n**Background/Objectives** : Obesity remains a global health challenge. Many commercial online weight loss programs are available, and they have advantages in terms of scalability and access. Few of these programs have been evaluated for effectiveness in a real-world context. This study reports on the weight loss achieved, platform engagement, and characteristics of successful weight loss predictions in subscribers to the Interval Weight Loss (IWL) program. The Interval Weight Loss program promotes intermittent restricted eating in addition to lifestyle changes in diet composition, exercise, and sleep. **Methods** : Data for 1705 adults subscribing to the program for >30 days between 2019 and 2024 were included in the analysis. A linear mixed model with polynomial terms was used to model weight loss over time with interaction terms for gender and age. Survival analysis was used to model the proportions and time frame of those meeting 2%, 5%, and 10% weight loss targets and the proportion meeting their goal weight. The focus of the analysis was on the effect at 365 days. Descriptive data from a subset of participants (n = 205) who completed a questionnaire about change in lifestyle habits and mood are also presented. **Results** : Of those who stayed in the program for at least 365 days, 25.4% achieved their goal weight, 17.6% achieved a 10% weight loss, and 62% achieved a 5% weight loss. By 49 days, 50% had lost 2% of their weight. Significant interactions indicated that males and females in their 60s and 70s were the most responsive to the program. **Conclusions** : The online commercial Interval Weight Loss platform based on intermittent restricted eating resulted in significant weight loss in a cohort of subscribers in a real-world setting.\n**Keywords:** online weight loss, intermittent restricted eating, commercial weight loss program\n##  1. Introduction\nObesity continues to be a global health issue. Among adults (over the age of 18), 43% of individuals are overweight, and 16% of individuals have obesity [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B1-nutrients-17-00332)]. Online and mobile weight loss platforms have been shown to be successful in promoting weight loss [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B2-nutrients-17-00332),[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B3-nutrients-17-00332)] and represent a cost-effective, scalable, and accessible alternative to attending in-person programs [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B4-nutrients-17-00332)]. In Australia, online weight loss programs represent 29.8% of the AUD 493.3 million weight loss industry in 2024 [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B5-nutrients-17-00332)].\nThis study outlines the weight loss results of the Interval Weight Loss program ([www.intervalweightloss.com](http://www.intervalweightloss.com)), a commercial online weight loss program based on the principle of intermittent restricted eating (IRE). IRE is a form of weight loss advice advocating alternating periods of weight loss and weight maintenance. The theory behind intermittent restricted eating focuses on avoiding the metabolic, endocrinologic, and behavioural adaptations [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B6-nutrients-17-00332)] that occur with sustained energy reduction. Intermittent restricted eating is a different approach to intermittent fasting, which involves fasting or time-restricted eating, in that the IRE approach advocates periods where habitual intake is restored and alternates with periods of energy restriction.\nA recent systematic review and meta-analysis found that while IRE did not result in significantly greater changes in body composition when compared with continuous energy restriction, IRE provided significant metabolic benefits in terms of preserving energy expenditure [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B7-nutrients-17-00332)]. All the studies considered in the review were randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and do not represent every-day or commercial contexts. As such, this research investigates the effectiveness of IRE in a real-world setting. This study provides novel descriptive data and addresses the hypotheses that subscribers to the program for \u2265365 days would achieve significant weight loss and that clinically significant weight loss would be achieved by a proportion of subscribers consistent with other weight loss interventions related in the literature.\nIn addition, we hypothesised that gender and age may have an effect on the weight loss achieved and that following the program would have a positive effect on behavioural change and mood.\nThe objectives of this research addressing the hypotheses are as follows:\n  1. Describe the participation patterns and dropout of subscribers to the IWL program;\n  2. Characterise the average long-term weight loss of subscribers;\n  3. Describe the success rates in terms of the proportion of subscribers who achieve their goal weight and the proportions losing 2%, 5%, and 10% of their starting weight;\n  4. Identify the recorded attributes associated with greater weight loss;\n  5. Describe the self-reported behavioural changes in response to the program.\n\n##  2. Materials and Methods\n### 2.1. The Interval Weight Loss Program\nThe program commenced in 2019; the theoretical framework is based on the results of the Matador study [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B8-nutrients-17-00332)]. The program advocates alternating a one-month period of weight loss with a one-month period of weight maintenance. In addition, the program advocates good exercise and sleep habits. The IWL has six principles: 1. You can\u2019t fight evolution: Impose weight loss breaks every second month to allow your body to adjust to it\u2019s new set point. 2. Overcoming food addiction: Reach for nature first: Retrain your brain and swap processed goods for nature\u2019s treats 3. Nutrition: The full rainbow: Eat more vegetables than you are used to and fill your plate with vegetables first. 4. Portion control: Use chopsticks: Eat big to small throughout the day and use a teaspoon, oyster fork or chopsticks with your evening meal to slow down the pace of eating. 5. Exercise: Choose to move: Incorporate incidental activity into your daily routine and focus on variety during weight loss months. 6. Better sleep: No blue light after twilight: Switch off all technology for two hours before bed and focus on hobbies such as reading, or tackle your to-do list. The IWL program focuses on improving diet, exercise, and sleep patterns.\nThe online IWL program offers a free trial period of 30 days. The main consideration of this analysis is long-term weight change in subscribers remaining in the program past this free period, with at least two valid weight entries. The focus is on those who remained in the program for \u2265365 days. Participants were individuals who subscribed to the program of their own volition between 20 August 2019 and 5 March 2024. Data were self-reported and entered onto the online platform. The variables for this analysis included information on weight, height, BMI, goal weight, waist circumference, age, gender, and program focus. The program has two levels: IWL and IWLPlus. IWL provides access to the online material through the app and/or website with daily reminders (optout) and individualised advice based on entered data. IWLPlus provides additional access to a dietitian via unlimited email contact and in-app chat and a monthly video or phone call for an additional cost. The program is billed quarterly or annually.\nA subset of participants also completed a check-in questionnaire about changes in lifestyle habits and program satisfaction.\n### 2.2. Data Collection\nApproval to conduct this research was received from the University of Wollongong Human Research Ethics Committee (HE2024/017). Subscribers agreed to a privacy policy allowing for their data to be used for research purposes. Data were deidentified, and participants were assigned a unique identifier prior to analysis.\n### 2.3. Statistical Methods\nDescriptive statistics are presented using the mean (SD) or median (IQR) as indicated. The main analysis for weight change over time was assessed using a linear mixed model with polynomial terms. AIC and BIC were used to determine the highest-order polynomial. All analysis was conducted in RStudio using R version 4.4.0 (\u201cPuppy Cup\u201d). Gender, age, and interaction terms for gender and time and age and time were included as covariates in the model. Survival analysis was used to determine the proportions of participants achieving 2% weight loss (percentage shown to predict success in trials as early weight loss [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B9-nutrients-17-00332)]), 5% weight loss (as this weight loss has been shown to be clinically significant [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B10-nutrients-17-00332)]), 10% weight loss, and the time to reach goal weight. Ten percent was chosen as this is a highly cited goal for long-term weight loss, which is reported to be met by only ~20% of people at one year [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B11-nutrients-17-00332)]. Three analyses are reported for each percentage weight loss criteria: 1. Where only participants remaining in the program at that time point are considered. This is the main analysis as it answers the question about the weight loss targets at \u2265365 days. This could be considered a type of on-treatment analysis. 2. The survival analysis with censoring. 3. An analysis where dropouts remain in the dataset with their last recorded weight carried forward (worst case). The differences between the base IWL program and IWLPlus were considered using a RMANOVA with an interaction term for program type and using Wilcoxon tests for within-group change and between-group differences. Due to the limited number of reported waist circumference measures, the change in waist circumference from the first to final measure was analysed using a Friedman test. An ANOVA was used to compare the difference in weight loss between the focus of the IWL principles at baseline. A McNemar\u2019s test was used to compare the change in proportions overtime for negative and positive feelings in the check-in survey. Given that we would perform 43 tests for the primary analysis in [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#nutrients-17-00332-t001), the interactions in [Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#nutrients-17-00332-t002), and the predictions in [Table 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#nutrients-17-00332-t003), an alpha level of 0.0012 was considered significant using a Bonferroni adjustment, using the standard deviation of the weight change reported from a previous study investigating weight change in an online program [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11768033/#B12-nutrients-17-00332)]. A total of 1705 subscribers allowed us to detect a change as low as 0.46 kg.\n#### Table 1.\nDescriptive statistics outlining subscription length and activity, goal weight, weight change, and BMI outputs\codex_findings\v27_deep_content_audit\_codex_stdout.txt:3102:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_TEST_072.json:8039:      "content": "Title: The Effects of Combined Fasting and Exercise on Inflammatory Cytokine Concentrations in Healthy Adults: A Randomized Crossover Study\n\nURL Source: https://digitalcommons.wku.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=4309&context=ijes\n\nPublished Time: Tue, 21 Oct 2025 23:14:56 GMT\n\nNumber of Pages: 17\n\nMarkdown Content:\nOriginal Research \n\n## The Effects of Combined Fasting and Exercise on Inflammatory Cytokine \n\n## Concentrations in Healthy Adults: A Randomized Crossover Study \n\nLandon S. Deru \u20201,2 , Austin F. Duersch* 1, Spencer C. Cleverly* 1, Hunter D. Chamberlain* 1,\n\nElizabeth Z. Gipson* 1, Parker G. Graves* 1, Cameron G. Jacobsen* 1, Larry A. Tucker \u20211 Bruce W. \n\nBailey \u20211 \n\n> 1\n\nDepartment of Exercise Sciences, Brigham Young University, Provo, U T, USA ; 2Division of \n\nPhysical Activity and Weight Management, University of Kansas Medical Center, Kansas City, \n\nKS, USA \n\n*Denotes student investigator, \u2020Denotes early -career investigator , \u2021Denotes e stablished investigator \n\nAbstract \n\nInternational Journ al of Exercise Science 18 (6): 1252 -1268 , 2025 .\n\nhttps://doi.org/ 10.70252/APHT9483 The o bject was t o assess the effects of adding exercise to a 36 -hour \n\nfast on the inflammatory cytokines IL -6, TNF -\u03b1, and MCP -1. The study was a r andomized crossover design with \n\ncounterbalanced conditions in c ommunity members living near a single academic institution. Participants included \n\ntwenty healthy adults (11 male). The i ntervention consisted of t wo 36 -h water -only fasts, one of which was initiated \n\nwith a bout of treadmill exercise. Venous blood was taken at baseline, 12 -h, 24 -h, and 36 -h of fasting. Area under \n\nthe curve and timepoint analyses were computed. The area under the curve for MCP -1 was 210.4 \u00b1 61.4 pg/ml \n\nhigher in th e fasting combined with exercise condition compared to fasting alone (F = 4.69, p = 0.04). No difference \n\nbetween conditions was observed in areas under the curve for IL -6 (F = 0.02, p = 0.88) or TNF -\u03b1 (F = 3.74, p = 0.06). \n\nMCP -1 concentrations decreased over the course of both conditions (F=19.77, p < 0.01) with much of the reduction \n\ntaking place between hours 12 and 24 (F=19.77, p < 0.01). Concentrations of IL -6 remained unchanged (F = 0.85, p \n\n= 0.48) while TNF -\u03b1 increased (F=8.60, p < 0.05) in both conditions. A single fast has a mixed impact on the \n\ncytokines MCP -1, TNF -\u03b1 and IL -6. MCP -1 decr eases, while TNF -\u03b1 fluctuates in a diurnal pattern, and IL -6\n\nexperiences no change during a water -only fast. Adding exercise to the beginning of a fast diminishes the decline \n\nin MCP -1 but has no impact on TNF -\u03b1 or IL -6. \n\nKeywords : Cytokines, intermittent fasting, behavioral intervention, physical activity \n\nIntroduction \n\nWith caloric intake, the body experiences metabolic strain that induces endocrine and \n\nimmunological responses to process food and restore homeostasis. 1 Due to modern eating \n\npatterns and food availability, most people spend more than 16 hours per day in a fed state \n\nwhich can lead to chronic postprandial -inflammation. 2 While inflammation is an essential \n\nresponse of our body's defense mechanisms and recovery, a chronic state of systemic low grade -Int J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1253 \n\ninflammation has been linked to obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer, and \n\nother debilitating ailments. 3,4 \n\nThe inflammatory processes in the body are mediated, in part, by cytokines. Cytokines are \n\nreleased from nearly every cell in the body and encompass a broad array of proteins involved \n\nin cell -signaling pathways that reduce or induce inflammation through enhancing immune \n\nfunction. Proinflammatory cytokines include Interleukin 6 (IL -6), Tumor Necrosis Factor alpha \n\n(TNF -\u03b1), Monocyte Chemoattractant Protein -1 (MCP -1), and a host of others. 5 Behavioral \n\nstrategies that reduce proinflammatory cytokines may help minimize inflammation and reduce \n\nthe incidence of chronic illnesses. Among such strategies, both fasting and exercise have been \n\nindependently proposed as effective means of lowering infla mmation. 6\n\nThe opposite is true of many foods found in the modern Western diet, some of which appear to \n\nspecifically induce inflammation. 7 It follows that fasting provides respite from these changes \n\nand reduces the associated inflammatory cytokines. 8 A reduction of inflammatory cytokines has \n\nbeen observed when subjects are maintained on a fasting regimen for several weeks. 9\n\nAdditionally, acute fasting (defined here as a single bout of complete caloric restriction) has \n\ndemonstrated a reduction in circulating monocyte levels, including CD14+ and CD16+, after 22 \n\nhours of water -only fasting 10 , a beneficial response as pro -inflammatory cells are linked to \n\nchronic inflammation and increase risk of cardiometabolic disease. 11 \n\nIn addition to fasting, exercise interventions have been effective in lowering inflammation. \n\nRegular long -term patterns of physical activity have been linked to a systemic reduction of \n\noxidative stress and inflammation including reduced levels of IL -6, TNF -\u03b1, and MCP -1. 9,12 \n\nImmediately following an acute bout of exercise, IL -6 concentrations increase which inhibits an \n\nendotoxin -induced increase in plasma TNF -\u03b1.13 While exercise may cause damage to contracting \n\nskeletal muscles and increase oxidative stress, Dimitrov, et al. suggests that increased \n\ncatecholamine levels due to sympathetic nervous system activation in exercise may also inhibit \n\nthe production of TNF -\u03b1.14 Similar suppressive effects of exercise have been noted in MCP -1\n\nconcentrations after 3 months of consistent exercise when performed 3 days per week 12 , but \n\nacute bouts of exercise may not have similar effects. Although multi -week intermittent -fasting \n\nregimens yield mixed effects on inflammation, several studies indicate that acute fasting can \n\nrapidly influence inflammatory biology. A single 24 -h fast reduces basal cytokine expression in \n\nadipose tissue and plasma (e.g., IL -1\u03b2, IL -6, TNF -\u03b1) in preclinical models. 15 In humans, short -\n\nterm fasting decreases the number and inflammatory activation state of circulating monocytes, \n\nwith refeeding inducing a transient rebound. 10,16 Observational and interventional work during \n\nRamadan, an ecologically valid time -restricted fasting model, has reported reductions in IL -6, \n\nTNF -\u03b1, and CRP in many cohorts. 17 These data provide a mechanistic and translational basis to \n\ntest whether an acute fasting bout of 36 hours alters circulating inflammatory markers in our \n\ntarget population and if an initial bout of exercise alters these outcomes. \n\nMultiple studies have evaluated the effects of fasting and exercise on inflammation \n\nindependently, often using fasting protocols such as time restricted eating, intermittent fasting, \n\nor caloric restriction. 18,19 These fasting protocols still involve some degree of caloric intake, \n\nwhich may explain the variability in outcomes. However, there is a paucity of work specifically Int J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1254 \n\nevaluating the combined effects of acute water -only fasting and exercise on inflammatory \n\nresponses. The purpose of this secondary analysis was to evaluate the effects of combining \n\nexercise and fasting on the inflammatory cytokines TNF -\u03b1, IL -6, and MCP -1. These cytokines \n\nwere measured in a 36 -h water -only fast and compared with those of a 36 -h fast initia ted with a \n\nbout of treadmill exercise. Water -only fasting was selected to eliminate the confounding \n\nmetabolic effects of nutrient intake and to isolate the physiological consequences of complete \n\nenergy deprivation on immune signaling. We hypothesized that an acute fast would begin to \n\ndampen proinflammatory markers and that adding a component of exercise would amplify \n\nthese changes. \n\nMethods \n\nBecause this is a secondary analysis, the methods for this study have been published \n\npreviously. 20 A summary of the methods is provided for context. A randomized crossover \n\ndesign with counterbalanced treatment conditions was used to compare the influence of fasting \n\nalone to fasting combined with vigorous exercise on the production of pro -inflammatory \n\ncytokines. These two conditions included a 36 -h water -only fast beginning at 8:00 PM and \n\nending at 8:00 AM, 36 -h later. 21 Approval from the university\u2019s institutional review board was \n\nobtained before initiating any aspect of this study (IRB2019 -319). This research was carried out \n\nfully in accordance with the ethical standards of the International Journal of Exercise Science. 22 \n\nParticipants completed two treatment conditions on identical days of the week, with a minimum \n\n6-d washout between each session. Condition order was randomly assigned to participant \n\nnumbers by the primary investigator before the study began. 23 Before each laboratory session, \n\nparticipants were screened for contraindications to participation as outlined hereinafter. The \n\noutcome variables measured were body mass index (BMI), percent body fat, fat mass, and \n\nplasma IL -6, TNF -\u03b1, and MCP -1 concentrati ons. \n\nParticipants \n\nThirty -one potential participants were screened for the study. Eleven were disqualified for \n\nreasons outlined in Figure 1. The remaining 20 individuals (11 male and 9 female) were \n\nrandomly allocated to condition order. The demographic characteristics of the participants are \n\noutlined in Table 1. \n\nAll participants were capable of participating in vigorous physical activity without restrictions \n\nas measured by a Physical Activity Readiness Questionnaire (PAR -Q). 24 Any \u201cyes\u201d response on \n\nthe PAR -Q excluded the participants. \n\nPotential participants were excluded from the study if they:  \n\n> \u2022\n\nwere diagnosed with a metabolic disease, inflammatory disease, orthopedic impairment, \n\neating disorder or food allergy;  \n\n> \u2022\n\nwere taking medications that altered metabolism;  \n\n> \u2022\n\nwere taking anti -inflammatory medications;  \n\n> \u2022\n\nwere consuming 60 mg or more of caffeine daily;  \n\n> \u2022\n\nwere pregnant, lactating, or postmenopausal; Int J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1255  \n\n> \u2022\n\ndid not have a BMI between 18.5 and <30 kg \u00b7m\u22122; \n\n> \u2022\n\nwere practicing ketogenic, carbohydrate, or calorie -restricted diets and/or  \n\n> \u2022\n\nwere not over the age of 18 years old.   \n\n> Figure 1 . Participant flow diagram\n> Table 1. Demographic characteristics of participants\n\nProtocol \n\nMeasurements .\n\nAt the beginning of each session, body weight and height were assessed. Weight was obtained \n\nusing a digital scale (Seca, Hamburg, Germany) with a precision of \u00b10.1 kg , while participants                                                              \n\n> Male (n=11) Female (n=9) Cumulative (n=20)\n> Mean SD Mean SD Mean SD\n> Age (years) 26.5 7.1 25.8 4.3 26.2 5.8\n> BMI (kg/m 2)24.7 3.1 22.7 3.6 23.8 3.4\n> Body Fat % 18.8 6.3 27.8 4.5 22.9 7.1\n> Visceral Adipose (g) 443.6 275.5 133.5 116.6 304 265.9\n> Exercise time (min) 49.3 5.9 54.7 7.8 51.7 7.2\n> Ethnicity n%n%n%\n> Asian 19222 315\n> Caucasian 873 778 15 75\n> Hawaiian/Pacific Islander 218 00210\n\nInt J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1256 \n\nwore athletic shorts and a T -shirt, standing barefoot. Height was measured by a stadiometer \n\naccurate to \u00b10.1 cm (Seca). Body composition, including fat mass, body fat percentage, and \n\nvisceral adipose tissue, was evaluated with a GE iDXA scanner (GE, Fairfi eld, CT). 25 Each \n\ntesting day began with calibration of the iDXA device using a calibration block supplied by the \n\nmanufacturer. All scans were processed using Encore software version 17. \n\nInflammatory Cytokines. \n\nVenipuncture occurred every 12 -h (baseline, 12, 24, and 36 -h) to collect two 4 -mL EDTA tubes \n\nof blood. Each 4 -mL tube was inverted to allow for mixture and both samples were centrifuged \n\nfor 15 min at 1500 g at 3\u00b0C within 10 min of collection. The plasma was then aliquoted and placed \n\nin separate vials and stored in a \u221280\u00b0F freezer until ready for analysis. IL -6, MCP -1, and TNF -\u03b1\n\nlevels were quantified using standard 96 -well microplate multiplex kits (MilliporeSigma, \n\nCatalog # HMHEMAG -34K). The manufacturer lists the percent coefficients variation (%CV) as \n\nfollows: IL -1 intra -assay %CV <10% and inter -assay %CV <15%; MCP -1 intra -assay %CV <10% \n\nand inter -assay %CV <15%; and TNF -\u03b1 intra -assay %CV <10% and inter -assay %CV <15%. \n\nOrientation. \n\nPrior to engaging in any part of the study, all participants provided informed consent and were \n\nthoroughly briefed on the study\u2019s overall objective and the procedures involved. They were \n\ninstructed to maintain their usual daily routines and sleep habits th roughout the testing period \n\nto minimize variability. Additionally, participants were asked to abstain from consuming \n\ncaffeine or other stimulants on the day of testing and to avoid strenuous physical activity during \n\nthe 24 hours leading up to their session . At the start of each visit, compliance with these \n\nguidelines was confirmed verbally by reiterating these instructions and asking the participant if \n\nthey were compliant. To further verify consistency across testing days, baseline levels of insulin \n\nand glu cagon were measured and showed no significant differences, indicating that participants \n\nbegan each session in a comparable metabolic condition. 20 Any deviations from the prescribed \n\npretest protocols would have resulted in the session being rescheduled. No deviations were \n\nrecorded during these pre -screenings. \n\nTreatment sessions. \n\nParticipants were instructed to follow their typical eating habits in the days leading up to the \n\nfasting period but were required to refrain from consuming any food for four hours prior to the \n\nstandardized meal. This approach was designed to prevent intent ional overconsumption before \n\nfasting and to ensure consistency in baseline blood measurements. 26 At the beginning of the \n\ninitial session, consent was obtained, and participants underwent orientation and received \n\ninstructions. Also, within this initial visit, anthropometrics, demographic data, and a baseline \n\nblood sample were obtained. After completin g these preliminary procedures, participants were \n\nprovided with a standardized meal. The completion of this meal marked the beginning of a 36 -\n\nhour water -only fast, during which no food or beverages \u2014caloric or non -caloric \u2014were \n\npermitted (with the exeption of water), including gum or mints. Follow -up blood samples were \n\ntaken at 12, 24, and 36 hours into the fasting period. Each participant was randomly assigned to Int J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1257 \n\nbegin with either an exercise or nonexercise condition. In the nonexercise condition, participants \n\nresumed their typical daily routines after consuming the standardized meal and completing \n\nmeasurements. In contrast, during the exercise condition, participa nts engaged in a prescribed \n\nworkout session beginning 30 minutes post -meal, following the same sequence of \n\nmeasurements. 27 \n\nStandardized meals. \n\nParticipants were given the same standardized meal to initiate each fast. The energy needs for \n\neach participant were estimated using height, weight, age, and sex to predict basal metabolic \n\nrates 28 , and an activity factor of 1.55 was used to estimate total daily energy requirements. 29 An \n\nactivity factor of 1.55 was used to represent a \u201cmoderate/low -active\u201d energy requirement \n\nrepresenting individuals who are sedentary at work or school but who undertake moderate \n\nphysical activity 1 -3 times per week. This level was verified for adults ages 18 -64 by Black et al. \n\nas a general representation of healthy adults in affluent societies such as the sample tested in \n\nthis study. 30 Meals were standardized based on macronutrient content consisting of 60% CHO, \n\n25% fat, 15% protein and consisted of combinations of raw almonds, string cheese, crackers, \n\napple slices, beef jerky, and commercially packaged peanut butter and jelly sandwich es. Meals \n\nwere assembled using both commercially available prepackaged foods and commercially \n\navailable boxed foods that were weighed to match caloric and macronutrient requirements of \n\nthe participant. Participants were given 25% (basal metabolic rate 1.55 0.25) of their daily caloric \n\nrequirements in the standardized meal. The same foods were given on both test days, and \n\nparticipants were instructed to consume all the food provided for each meal with meal \n\nadherence being assessed in each session by direct o bservation. The standardized meal fed to \n\nparticipants before each fast was 614.8 \u00b1 85.2 kcal. \n\nExercise protocol. \n\nParticipants exercised on a treadmill at a grade and speed that brought their estimated heart rate \n\nreserve (HRR) to 70%. 31 The grade and speed combinations were made based on the preference \n\nof the participant to increase heart rate (HR). Participants exercised in this manner until an \n\nequivalent number of calories was expended as given in the standardized meal in an attempt to \n\ncreate a calorie neutral start to the fast. The formula used to calculate the participant\u2019s target \n\nheart rate was as follows: 32 \n\nTarget HR = [%target intensity \u00d7 (HR max \u2212 HR rest )] + HR rest [1] \n\nMaximal HR estimation was calculated using the following formula: 33 \n\nHR max = 208 \u2212 (0.7 \u00d7 age) [2] \n\nParticipants were fitted with a strap -on heart rate monitor (Garmin, Olathe, KS) and instructed \n\nto be seated for 5 min to establish resting HR (HR rest ). Once HR calculations were complete, \n\nsubjects began the exercise. The speed and grade were adjusted to meet the target HR within \n\nthe first 5 min of exercise. Once this speed and grade were set, they were not adjusted for the Int J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1258 \n\nremainder of the exercise intervention. Two participants were unable to maintain the exercise at \n\nthis intensity, they were allowed to take a 60 -s break, after which the exercise was resumed. \n\nThe length of exercise was individualized and calculated to expend a similar number of calories \n\nto that consumed from the standardized meal. This calculation was based on the standard \n\nAmerican College of Sports Medicine \u2013established metabolic calculation co nverting oxygen to \n\ncalories by multiplying liters of oxygen by 5. The equations that were used include: \n\nAmerican College of Sports Medicine metabolic equation: \n\nV\u00b7 O2 mL \u00b7kg \u22121\u00b7 min \u22121 = 0.2 S + 0.9 SG + 3.5 [3] \n\nEquations to estimate time on treadmill: \n\nMinutes of exercise = ( E/5 \u00d7 1000)/(kg \u00d7 (0.2 S + 0.9 SG + 3.5) [4] \n\nwhere \n\nE = calculated energy based on standardized meal in kilocalories \n\nkg = weight of the participant in kilograms \n\nS = speed of the treadmill in meters per minute \n\nG = grade of the treadmill min = time on treadmill \n\nAll calculations were performed in a preset, protected spreadsheet to ensure accuracy. Indirect \n\ncalorimetry was used continuously throughout the exercise to verify energy expenditure \n\n(COSMED, Rome, Italy). Measured energy expenditure during the exercise b out on the fast and \n\nexercise day averaged 587.6 \u00b1 120.1 kcal. The average METs during the prescribed exercise were \n\n9.14 \u00b1 1.37. The average respiratory quotient (R) (measured by indirect calorimetry) throughout \n\nthe prescribed exercise was 0.95 \u00b1 0.14. \n\nStatistical Analysis \n\nData were analyzed using SAS software version 9.4 for Windows. The sample size for this study \n\nwas calculated a priori using an alpha of 0.05, and conservatively estimating a 10% difference in \n\nTNF -\u03b1 concentrations between conditions. This provided a moder ate effect size of 0.66. A\n\nsample of 20 participants was needed to yield 80% power. \n\nEvaluation of the impact of fasting over time was performed using a general linear mixed model \n\nand follow up tests were conducted using differences of least square means. Analysis was \n\nperformed between individual time segments of fasting intervention at wh ich venipuncture was \n\ncompleted (0 vs 12 -h, 12 vs 24 -h, 24 vs 36 -h). \n\nArea under the curve (AUC) analysis was completed to compare cytokine concentrations \n\nbetween conditions. AUC estimations were calculated using trapezoidal sums with one Int J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1259 \n\nobservation per subject by treatment with area under the curve as the dependent variable. The \n\narea under the curve was computed to represent a total response with a single number as a \n\nmeasure of intensity of the response and the magnitude of the change bet ween conditions. \n\nPlasma cytokine concentrations were examined using repeated measures mixed model \n\nANOVA. Where there was a significant interaction between time and a cytokine concentration \n\nbetween conditions, a post hoc least significant difference pairwis e comparison was used to \n\nexamine significant differences. Baseline values were used as a covariate. All results are \n\npresented as means \u00b1 SD. The data used to support the results of this study are available in the \n\nOpen Science Framework repository. 34 \n\nResults \n\nSex differences \n\nThere were no significant differences between sexes in their response to the intervention. \n\nSpecifically, there was no significant sex by condition interaction for any measured cytokines \n\n(MCP -1; 208.96 \u00b1 65.93 pg/ml, F=2.99, p = 0.1029; TNF -\u03b1; 15.76 \u00b1 9.56 pg/ml, F=.15, p = 0.7031). \n\nBecause no differences in sex were observed, men and women were then analyzed together. \n\nArea under the curve between conditions \n\nThere was a significant difference between conditions for MCP -1 over time course of the fast. \n\nThe difference in area under the curve for MCP -1 was 210.4 \u00b1 61.4 pg/ml (F = 4.69, p = 0.0369) \n\nwith MCP -1 having a greater area under the curve during the fasting intervention preceded by \n\na bout of treadmill exercise (See Table 2). No significant difference was observed between areas \n\nunder the curve for IL -6 (28.0 \u00b1 182.4 pg/ ml, F= 0.02, p = 0.8807) or TNF -\u03b1 (16.1 \u00b1 9.3 pg/ml, F= \n\n3.02 , p = 0.1003).                          \n\n> Table 2. Area under the curve for inflammatory cytokines over a 36 -hour fast started with or without exercise.\n> Condition AUC (pg/ml \u00d7hours) F-value P-value\n> MCP -1No Exercise 2501.5 \u00b1 141.0* 4.69 0.0369\n> Exercise 2711.9 \u00b1 140.9\n> TNF -\u03b1No Exercise 195.0 \u00b1 5.01 3.74 0.0608\n> Exercise 211.2 \u00b1 5.02\n> IL -6No Exercise 8738.6 \u00b1 116.8 0.02 0.8807\n> Exercise 8766.7 \u00b1 116.9\n> MCP -1 = Monocyte chemoattractant protein -1, TNF -\u03b1= Tumor necrosis factor alpha, IL -6 = Interleukin 6.\n> *Statistical difference between conditions for area under the curve. Results are reported as mean \u00b1 the standard\n> deviation\n\nPlasma MCP -1 concentrations saw a significant overall reduction over the course of the fast in \n\nboth conditions (F=19.77, p < 0.0001; See Table 3). This reduction in MCP -1 took place primarily \n\nbetween hours 12 and 24 and then remained low between hours 24 and 36 (Figure 2). Plasma \n\nconcentrations of IL -6 (Figure 4) remained similar over the course of the fast (F = 0.85, p = 0.4768). \n\nThere was no difference between conditions observed at any time point (See Table 3). TNF -\u03b1\n\nincreased slightly over the course o f the non -exercise fast (F=8.60, p = 0.0463) but the change \n\nwas not linear. Concentrations increased over the first 12 hours of the fast (F=8.60, p < 0.0001), Int J Exerc Sci 18 (6): 12 52 -12 68 , 2025 \n\nInternational Journal of Exercise Science http://www.intjexersci.com \n\n1260 \n\nthen decreased from hours 12 to 24 and then went up again from hours 24 to 36 (Figure 3). When \n\ncomparing the values of TNF -\u03b1 between conditions at the same time of the day, there were no \n\ndifferences with the exception that hour 36 was lower than hour 12 in the exercise condition (See \n\nTable 3). \n\nTable 3.
